item.title: OneSignal vs. Direct Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) Implementation
item.author: Aishwarya M S
item.content:encoded: Push notifications are a critical feature for engaging users in mobile applications. While Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) provides a direct way to send notifications, OneSignal acts as a middleware, offering additional features like automation, analytics, and segmentation. This documentation explores the differences between using OneSignal and directly implementing FCM.What is Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM)?FCM is Google’s free messaging service that allows developers to send notifications and messages to Android, iOS, and web applications. It provides APIs to send targeted messages based on device tokens.OneSignal is a third-party push notification service that simplifies the integration of push notifications across multiple platforms. It uses FCM for Android but provides additional features like user segmentation, analytics, and automation.Key Differences: OneSignal vs. Direct FCM ImplementationWhen to Use Direct FCM Implementation?If you only need basic notifications without analytics and automation.If you over the messaging logic.If your app is and you don’t need cross-platform support.If you prefer not to depend on third-party services.How to Implement FCM Directly?Create a Firebase Project (Firebase Console)Register your Android/iOS App in Firebase. (for Android) or GoogleService-Info.plist (for iOS) and place it in the appropriate directories. to register devices and send notifications.If you need cross-platform notifications (Android, iOS, Web).If you want automation, analytics, and audience segmentation.If you prefer an easier setup with a UI-based configuration.If you don’t want to manage Firebase tokens manually.How to Integrate OneSignal with Firebase?Create a Firebase Project and enable FCM. and place it in /platforms/android/app/.Add FCM Server Key & Sender ID to OneSignal.Install OneSignal Cordova Plugin:cordova plugin add onesignal-cordova-plugindocument.addEventListener("deviceready", function() { window.plugins.OneSignal.initialize("YOUR_ONESIGNAL_APP_ID"); window.plugins.OneSignal.Notifications.requestPermission(true); }, false);For simple apps needing , direct FCM implementation is a viable choice. However, for a scalable, feature-rich, and easy-to-maintain solution, OneSignal is the better option. It abstracts the complexity of managing FCM tokens and handling multi-platform notifications, making it ideal for Cordova apps.Which one should you choose?If you prioritize , go with . If you need ease of integration, analytics, and cross-platform support, is the way to go.

item.title: Firebase Web Push Notifications
item.author: Aishwarya M S
item.content:encoded:
This documentation explains how to implement Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for web push notifications. It covers setting up Firebase, configuring the service worker, subscribing users to notifications, and sending push notifications.Step 1: Create a Firebase ProjectClick on and follow the instructions.Navigate to and select .Under “Your apps,” click > .Copy the Firebase config object for use in your project.Include the following Firebase SDK in your HTML file:Add the Firebase configuration in app.js:// Firebase Configurationsvar firebaseConfig = { projectId: "YOUR_PROJECT_ID", storageBucket: "YOUR_STORAGE_BUCKET", messagingSenderId: "YOUR_MESSAGING_SENDER_ID",};firebase.initializeApp(firebaseConfig);const messaging = firebase.messaging();3. Register Service WorkerCreate a file sw.js (service worker) with the following:importScripts('https://www.gstatic.com/firebasejs/7.14.0/firebase-app.js');importScripts('https://www.gstatic.com/firebasejs/7.14.0/firebase-messaging.js');firebase.initializeApp(firebaseConfig);const messaging = firebase.messaging();In app.js, register the service worker:if ('serviceWorker' in navigator) { navigator.serviceWorker.register('./sw.js').then(res => { console.log("Service Worker Registered"); messaging.useServiceWorker(res); console.log("Service Worker Registration Failed", err);}4. Subscribe to Push NotificationsTo request permission and get the user’s FCM token:const subscribe = () => { const token = document.getElementById('token'); Notification.requestPermission().then(permission => { if (permission === "granted") { messaging.getToken().then(currentToken => { console.log("FCM Token:", currentToken); token.textContent = currentToken; }); token.textContent = "Permission not granted"; }).catch(err => { console.log("Error getting permission", err); });5. Sending Push NotificationsTo send notifications, use Firebase Cloud Messaging API:

Send Push Notification

const sendPush = () => { const token = document.getElementById('usertoken').value; const title = document.getElementById('title').value; const body = document.getElementById('body').value; to: token, title: title, click_action: "/", }; fetch("https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send", { method: "POST", "Authorization": "key=YOUR_SERVER_KEY", "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify(payload) }) .then(data => { alert(data.failure ? "Token Expired" : "Notification Sent Successfully"); }) .catch(err => alert("Error: " + err));};6. Receiving Push NotificationsThe service worker listens for incoming push notifications:messaging.onMessage(payload => { console.log("Notification received: ", payload); new Notification(payload.notification.title, { body: payload.notification.body, });

item.title: How to Send Push Notifications in a Cordova App Using OneSignal and Firebase
item.author: Aishwarya M S
item.content:encoded: Push notifications are an essential feature in modern mobile applications, enabling real-time communication with users. Setting up push notifications in a app using and Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM).Why Use OneSignal with Firebase for Notifications?OneSignal is a powerful push notification service that simplifies implementation across multiple platforms. However, OneSignal still relies on Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for delivering notifications to Android devices. Here’s why we integrate OneSignal with Firebase:: OneSignal provides a simple API to send notifications to Android, iOS, and web apps.: It supports segmentation, automation, analytics, and personalization.: FCM is responsible for delivering push messages to Android devices, making it a necessary integration.: OneSignal handles device registration, subscription, and push token management automatically.A Cordova project set up.Firebase account (Firebase Console).Android SDK installed on your system.Step 1: Set Up Firebase and Download google-services.jsonCreate or select an existing project. to your Firebase project.Enter the (e.g., com.yourapp.name).Click and then .google-services.json file inside your Cordova project at:/projectfolder/google-services.jsonStep 2: Configure Firebase Cloud Messaging in OneSignalSelect your and navigate to .Click Google Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM).Copy the from Firebase:In Firebase, go to Project Settings > Cloud Messaging.Paste it in OneSignal’s field.5. Copy the from Firebase:In Firebase, go to Project Settings > Cloud Messaging.Copy the Sender ID (Project Number).Paste it in OneSignal’s field.Step 3: Add OneSignal to Your Cordova App3.1 Install OneSignal Cordova PluginRun the following command in your Cordova project directory:cordova plugin add onesignal-cordova-plugin3.2 Initialize OneSignal in JavaScriptAdd this inside your index.js or main script file:document.addEventListener("deviceready", function() { window.plugins.OneSignal.initialize("YOUR_ONESIGNAL_APP_ID"); window.plugins.OneSignal.Notifications.requestPermission(true); window.plugins.OneSignal.Notifications.addEventListener("click", function(event) { console.log("Notification Clicked:", event);}, false);Replace YOUR_ONESIGNAL_APP_ID with the App ID from your OneSignal dashboard.Step 4: Sending Push Notifications from OneSignal4.1 Sending Notifications via OneSignal DashboardGo to .Select your app and navigate to .Enter the and .Select the (all users or specific segments).4.2 Sending Notifications via OneSignal APIUse this PHP code to send push notifications programmatically:function sendNotification() { $app_id = 'YOUR_ONESIGNAL_APP_ID'; $api_key = 'YOUR_ONESIGNAL_REST_API_KEY'; 'app_id' => $app_id, 'include_player_ids' => ['USER_PLAYER_ID'], 'headings' => ['en' => 'Hello!'], 'contents' => ['en' => 'This is a test notification.'], ]; $fields = json_encode($data); $ch = curl_init('https://onesignal.com/api/v1/notifications'); curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER, [ 'Content-Type: application/json', 'Authorization: Basic ' . $api_key, curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_RETURNTRANSFER, true); curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_POST, true); curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_POSTFIELDS, $fields); $response = curl_exec($ch); curl_close($ch); echo 'Notification Sent: ' . $response;}YOUR_ONESIGNAL_APP_ID with your OneSignal App ID.YOUR_ONESIGNAL_REST_API_KEY with your OneSignal REST API Key.USER_PLAYER_ID with the recipient's unique player ID.Step 5: Handling Notifications in Your App5.1 Receiving NotificationsWhen a notification is received, add an event listener to handle it:window.plugins.OneSignal.Notifications.addEventListener("received", function(event) { console.log("Notification Received:", event);5.2 Handling Notification ClicksWhen users tap on a notification, navigate them accordingly:window.plugins.OneSignal.Notifications.addEventListener("click", function(event) { console.log("Notification Clicked:", event); localStorage.setItem('notification_data', JSON.stringify(event));});By integrating with Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM), you get a powerful push notification system with minimal setup. Firebase acts as the delivery backbone, while OneSignal provides an easy-to-use interface and advanced features like segmentation and automation.Google Firebase handles message delivery to Android devices.OneSignal simplifies integration by managing device registration and API calls. to enable Firebase services in Cordova.FCM Server Key & Sender ID must be configured in the OneSignal dashboard.This setup ensures seamless notification delivery and enhances user engagement in your Cordova app.

item.title: Bootstrap Auth Scaffolding -Laravel Authentication Setup
item.author: Aishwarya M S
item.content:encoded:
Laravel provides a built-in authentication system (php artisan ui bootstrap --auth) that user login and registration functionalities. If you don’t use Laravel’s migrations and authentication methods properly, the login and register system due to missing tables, incorrect authentication handling, or broken session management.Migrations Ensure Database Tables ExistLaravel’s authentication system relies on a users table.If the users table does not exist in the database, login and registration because Laravel won't be able to store or retrieve user credentials.Trying to register or log in without running migrations results in errors like:“Base table or view not found: 1146 Table ‘users’ doesn’t exist”.Migrations create all required tables, including:✅ users table (stores user data)✅ password_resets table (for password recovery)✅ failed_jobs, personal_access_tokens, etc. (for additional features)Laravel provides built-in authentication scaffoldingcomposer require laravel/uiphp artisan ui bootstrap --authThis command will generate:Controller for authenticationViews for login and registrationapp/Http/Controllers/Auth/LoginController.phpnamespace App\Http\Controllers\Auth;use App\Http\Controllers\Controller;use Illuminate\Http\Request;use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Auth;class LoginController extends Controller{ public function __construct() { $this->middleware('guest')->except('logout'); } public function showLoginForm() { return view('auth.login'); } public function login(Request $request) { 'email' => 'required|email', 'password' => 'required|min:6', ]); if (Auth::attempt(['email' => $request->email, 'password' => $request->password])) { return redirect()->intended('dashboard'); // Redirect to dashboard return back()->withErrors(['email' => 'Invalid Credentials']); } { return redirect('/login');}use App\Http\Controllers\Auth\LoginController;Route::get('/login', [LoginController::class, 'showLoginForm'])->name('login');Route::post('/login', [LoginController::class, 'login']);Route::post('/logout', [LoginController::class, 'logout'])->name('logout');Protect Routes (Middleware)To restrict access to certain pages, use the auth middleware. In routes/web.php:Route::get('/dashboard', function () { return view('dashboard');resources/views/dashboard.blade.php Dashboard

Welcome to the Dashboard

You are logged in!

@csrf
ensure that the necessary tables exist in the database.✅ Laravel’s authentication methods (Auth::attempt(), Hash::make()) handle user login securely.✅ and prevent security risks.✅ Running migrations and installing dependencies ensures the system functions properly.

item.title: Setting Up Laravel Base URL in Production Mode
item.author: Aishwarya M S
item.content:encoded: Run the following command to create a new Laravel project:composer create-project --prefer-dist laravel/laravel laravel2. Navigate to the Project Directory3. Start the Laravel Development Server (For Local Testing)4. Set Proper Permissions for Storage and Bootstrap CacheOn a production server, Laravel requires write permissions for the storage and bootstrap/cache directories. Run the following commands:sudo chmod -R 775 /var/www/html/laravel/storage /var/www/html/laravel/bootstrap/cachesudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html/laravel/storage /var/www/html/laravel/bootstrap/cache5. Set the Base URL in the .env FileOpen the .env file in the root directory and update the APP_URL variable:Update your .env file to use file or cookie instead of database:Update config/session.php'driver' => env('SESSION_DRIVER', 'file'),APP_URL=https://domain/laravel/public/6. Access the Welcome Page

item.title: Blue-Green Deployment with Jenkins, AWS ECR, and AWS EC2 Using Django
item.author: Jaecom
item.content:encoded: Learn how to set up a fully automated Blue-Green deployment pipeline for Django using Jenkins, AWS ECR, and EC2. is a DevOps strategy that minimizes downtime and risk during software releases. It involves two identical production environments: one active (Blue) and one standby (Green). While users interact with the active environment, you update and test the standby environment. Once it’s verified, you can seamlessly switch traffic to the newly updated environment, thus achieving zero-downtime deployments.In this article, we will create a Jenkins pipeline that:Builds and pushes a Dockerized Django application to (Elastic Container Registry).Deploys these Docker images to instances.Uses a Blue-Green approach to keep downtime minimal.By the end, you’ll have a basic CI/CD pipeline and a repeatable method for releasing Django application updates without interrupting your users.1.1 Install Jenkins PluginsMake sure you have the following Jenkins plugins installed beforehand:Configure Jenkins Credentials in Manage Jenkins → Manage Credentials. Here is an overview:After you’ve set up the credentials, in your Jenkins Console, you should see the following:This guide assumes a load balancer is already set up, directing traffic to both of your EC2 instances. If you don’t yet have a load balancer, you can still deploy to multiple servers, but you’ll need a different mechanism to switch traffic seamlessly.2.1 Create a Jenkinsfile with Defined DeploymentsFirst, define your EC2 targets in a list called deployments. Each entry includes:title: A descriptive name (used in the pipeline UI).name: The Docker container name for running on that server.credentialsId: The Jenkins credential ID containing the SSH key.host: The public DNS or IP of the EC2 instance.user: The SSH username (e.g., ubuntu on Ubuntu).port: The external port on the EC2 instance mapped to the Django container’s port (commonly 8000).def deployments = [ [ "name": 'project-server-1', "credentialsId": 'project-ec2-ssh', "host": '<ec2 url>', "port": '8000' [ "name": 'project-server-2', "credentialsId": 'project-ec2-ssh', "host": '<ec2 url>', "port": '8000']2.2 Define a Pipeline with Environment VariablesNext, create your pipeline and set environment variables for AWS:def deployments = [ //defined deployments from above agent any AWS_REGION = '<ap-northeast-2>' ECR_REGISTRY = '<your ecr registry>' ECR_REPO = '<ecr repo name>' //... add the stages as you continue this guide}AWS_REGION: Your AWS region (e.g., ap-northeast-2).ECR_REGISTRY: The URI for your ECR registry (e.g., 123456789012.dkr.ecr.ap-northeast-2.amazonaws.com).ECR_REPO: The name of your ECR repository (e.g., my-django-app).2.3 Retrieve Git Commit MessageIn the first stage, get the latest commit message to use as a Docker tag. Docker tags are limited to 128 characters, so you’ll truncate accordingly:stage('Get Git Commit Message') { steps { // Get the commit message, sanitize it, and save it as a new environment variable COMMIT_MESSAGE = sh(script: "git log -1 --pretty=%B | tr -d '\n' | tr ' ' '-' | tr -cd '[:alnum:]._-'", returnStdout: true).trim() env.COMMIT_TAG = COMMIT_MESSAGE.take(128) // Docker tags are limited to 128 characters } }Load your Django (or other application) .env variables from Jenkins Credentials:stage('Load Env File from Jenkins Credentials') { steps { withCredentials([file(credentialsId: 'project-server-env', variable: 'ENV_FILE')]) { // Copy the uploaded .env file to the project if [ -f .env ]; then fi sh 'cp $ENV_FILE .env' }campable-server-env: The Jenkins “Secret file” credential ID that holds your application’s .env.2.5 Stage: Build Docker ImageBuild the Docker image for your Django app, then push it to ECR:stage('Build Docker Image') { steps { withCredentials([[$class: 'AmazonWebServicesCredentialsBinding', credentialsId: 'project-aws-ecr-cred']]) { sh ''' aws ecr get-login-password --region ${AWS_REGION} | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${ECR_REGISTRY} ''' docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t ${ECR_REPO} . docker tag ${ECR_REPO}:latest ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker tag ${ECR_REPO}:latest ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:${COMMIT_TAG} docker push ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker push ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:${COMMIT_TAG} ''' }Finally, deploy the image to each EC2 instance. For each server in deployments, Jenkins will:SSH into the server using the specified credentials.Log in to ECR and pull the latest Docker image.Tag the pulled image locally.Stop and remove any existing container with the same name.Run the new container on the desired port.Prune old Docker images to save disk space.stage('SSH into EC2 and Pull Image') { steps { deployments.each { server -> stage("Deploy to ${server.title}") { withCredentials([[$class: 'AmazonWebServicesCredentialsBinding', credentialsId: 'project-aws-ecr-cred']]) { sshagent(credentials: [server.credentialsId]) { sh """ ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no ${server.user}@${server.host} " aws ecr get-login-password --region ${AWS_REGION} | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${ECR_REGISTRY} docker pull ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker tag ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest ${server.name} docker stop ${server.name} || true docker rm ${server.name} || true docker run -d -p ${server.port}:8000 --name ${server.name} --network campable-network ${server.name} docker image prune -f """ } } }Here’s the complete Jenkinsfile, combining all the stages:def deployments = [ [ "name": 'project-server-1', "credentialsId": 'project-ec2-ssh', "host": '<ec2 url>', "port": '8000' [ "name": 'project-server-2', "credentialsId": 'project-ec2-ssh', "host": '<ec2 url>', "port": '8000'] agent any AWS_REGION = '<ap-northeast-2>' ECR_REGISTRY = '<your ecr registry>' ECR_REPO = '<ecr repo name>' stage('Get Git Commit Message') { steps { // Get the commit message, sanitize it, and save it as a new environment variable COMMIT_MESSAGE = sh(script: "git log -1 --pretty=%B | tr -d '\n' | tr ' ' '-' | tr -cd '[:alnum:]._-'", returnStdout: true).trim() env.COMMIT_TAG = COMMIT_MESSAGE.take(128) // Docker tags are limited to 128 characters } } stage('Load Env File from Jenkins Credentials') { steps { withCredentials([file(credentialsId: 'project-server-env', variable: 'ENV_FILE')]) { // Copy the uploaded .env file to the project if [ -f .env ]; then fi sh 'cp $ENV_FILE .env' } stage('Build Docker Image') { steps { withCredentials([[$class: 'AmazonWebServicesCredentialsBinding', credentialsId: 'project-aws-ecr-cred']]) { sh ''' aws ecr get-login-password --region ${AWS_REGION} | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${ECR_REGISTRY} ''' docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t ${ECR_REPO} . docker tag ${ECR_REPO}:latest ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker tag ${ECR_REPO}:latest ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:${COMMIT_TAG} docker push ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker push ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:${COMMIT_TAG} ''' } stage('SSH into EC2 and Pull Image') { steps { deployments.each { server -> stage("Deploy to ${server.title}") { withCredentials([[$class: 'AmazonWebServicesCredentialsBinding', credentialsId: 'project-aws-ecr-cred']]) { sshagent(credentials: [server.credentialsId]) { sh """ ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no ${server.user}@${server.host} " aws ecr get-login-password --region ${AWS_REGION} | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin ${ECR_REGISTRY} docker pull ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest docker tag ${ECR_REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:latest ${server.name} docker stop ${server.name} || true docker rm ${server.name} || true docker run -d -p ${server.port}:8000 --name ${server.name} --network campable-network ${server.name} docker image prune -f """ } } } }3.1 Testing Blue-Green DeploymentTo confirm there’s during deployment, create a script that sends requests to your application endpoint every second. If your setup is correct, you’ll see continuous HTTP 200 (or your success code) responses throughout the update.Create a monitor_deployment.sh script:nano monitor_deployment.shPaste in the following (update TARGET_URL to your own health-check or application endpoint):#!/usr/bin/env bash# Paste in your Target URL for health checksTARGET_URL="https://your-api-endpoint.com/health"echo "Starting deployment monitor..."echo "Sending requests to: $TARGET_URL"echo "Interval: $SLEEP_INTERVAL second(s)"echo "Press Ctrl + C to stop." TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %T") HTTP_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$TARGET_URL") echo "[${TIMESTAMP}] - HTTP ${HTTP_CODE}"doneMake the script executable:chmod +x monitor_deployment.shYou should see something like this:Starting deployment monitor...Sending requests to: https://your-api-endpoint.com/healthPress Ctrl + C to stop.[2025-03-10 10:15:01] - HTTP 200[2025-03-10 10:15:02] - HTTP 200[2025-03-10 10:15:03] - HTTP 200...With the monitoring script running, push a new update (e.g., run your Jenkins pipeline again).If your Blue-Green deployment is configured correctly, you should see in your monitoring script logs (i.e., continuous 200 responses or whichever success code you expect).3.3 Verify Docker ContainersAfter the pipeline completes, SSH into each EC2 instance and confirm the new containers are running:A successful run might look like this://server 1CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES7b7s2v42ab3e project-server-1 "gunicorn --bind 0.0…" 1 min ago Up 1 min 0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp project-server-1//server 2CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES7b5f6e12ab3e project-server-2 "gunicorn --bind 0.0…" 1 min ago Up 1 min 0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp project-server-2If both containers are listed, and your monitoring script showed no failed requests, you’ve successfully performed a Blue-Green deployment with zero downtime!By following the steps in this guide, you have successfully created a pipeline using , , , and . Specifically, you:Defined Jenkins Environments to maintain consistent AWS configuration (region, ECR registry, and repository).Retrieved Git Commit Messages to generate meaningful Docker tags.Loaded Environment Variables (.env) from Jenkins Credentials for application-specific secrets.Built and Pushed Docker Images to ECR, leveraging AWS credentials for secure access.Deployed to EC2 Instances by pulling the newest image, stopping/removing old containers, and running the updated containers. by continuously monitoring your application endpoint with a simple Bash script.With this pipeline, you can confidently deploy new versions of your Django application without interrupting user traffic. Feel free to expand on these steps — by adding automated tests, integrating load balancer logic, or enhancing your monitoring approach — to tailor the solution to your specific production requirements.

item.title: Aptori Now on Google Cloud Marketplace for AI-Powered Security and Automated Risk Remediation

item.title: Announcing support for upgrades to Java 21 in Amazon Q Developer
item.author: Brian Beach
item.content:encoded: On February 14, Amazon Q Developer announced support for upgrades to Java 21. As a Java developer, I’m excited about this new capability, which will make it easier for me to keep my applications up-to-date and take advantage of the latest language features and performance improvements. In addition, the latest version of Amazon Q Developer includes improved summarization and recommendations that simplify the upgrade process and increase confidence in the results.Amazon Q Developer is a generative AI-powered assistant that helps accelerate the modernization of enterprise applications. It can perform complex tasks like analyzing legacy code, mapping dependencies, and executing migration and modernization workflows. Amazon Q Developer frees up your team to focus on more strategic initiatives rather than getting bogged down in the undifferentiated heavy lifting of upgrading Java applications.Staying current on Java versions is crucial, as each new release brings important security fixes, performance enhancements, and support for emerging frameworks and libraries. However, the effort required to manually migrate large Java codebases can be daunting. That’s where Amazon Q Developer has been invaluable. By offloading the tedious, labor-intensive parts of the upgrade process, your team can deliver important updates to users much faster while minimizing disruption.With the addition of Java 21 upgrade capabilities, Amazon Q Developer now supports upgrading applications from Java 8, 11, and 17 to Java 17 or 21. Some of the key benefits of Java 21 that I’m looking forward to include:Virtual Threads are a new concurrency primitive introduced in Java 19 that reduce the effort of writing, maintaining, and debugging high-throughput concurrent applications. This unlocks significant performance improvements for your applications.Better Memory Management: Enhancements to the Z Garbage Collector in Java 21 result in more predictable garbage collection pauses and reduced memory footprint, leading to more stable and responsive applications.Upgrading your team’s Java applications to Java 21 with Amazon Q Developer is a game-changer. It saves countless hours of manual effort that would otherwise be required to migrate all of your Java components.Simplifying the Upgrade Process with Amazon Q DeveloperUpgrading Java applications to Java 21 with Amazon Q Developer is easy. After configuring your project and ensuring it meets the prerequisites, you can simply invoke the command in the Amazon Q Developer chat window of your integrated development environment (IDE). The following screenshot is from VS Code; however, Q Developer also supports JetBrain’s IDEs including IntelliJ IDEA and the command line.Amazon Q Developer will then analyze your codebase, determine the necessary changes to upgrade to Java 21, and provide a detailed diff so you can review and accept the transformations. This not only saves you time, but also helps ensure a consistent, high-quality upgrade across all of your Java applications.In addition to adding support for upgrading to Java 21, the latest version of Amazon Q Developer also enhances the summarization and recommendations it provides after completing the transformation. Once the upgrade to Java 21 is complete, Q Developer generates a detailed summary of the changes made, such as deprecated APIs removed and code refactored to leverage new Java features. It also includes tailored recommendations to further improve the application, taking advantage of the capabilities introduced in Java 21. For example, Q Developer suggested upgrading the logging framework and implementing pattern matching to make the code more concise. These summary and recommendation capabilities help ensure a smooth and comprehensive upgrade process.Finally, Q includes recommendations to further improve the application beyond the upgrade to Java 21. For example, Q provided the following recommendations.The summary and recommendation capabilities help ensure a smooth and comprehensive upgrade experience. Developers can review the detailed changes, understand the rationale behind them, and then selectively apply the suggested optimizations to fully unlock the benefits of Java 21. This level of transparency and guidance from Q Developer greatly simplifies the upgrade process and increases confidence in the resulting codebase.In summary, the new Amazon Q Developer transformation capabilities for upgrades to Java 21 offload the labor-intensive task of keeping your Java applications up-to-date. The detailed summaries and tailored recommendations provided by Q Developer ensure a smooth and comprehensive upgrade process, and significantly streamline the upgrade process. I’m excited to start rolling this out and freeing up the team’s time to focus on higher-value work. If you’re a fellow Java developer, I’d highly encourage you to try out Amazon Q Developer for yourself. To get started, visit the Amazon Q Developer getting started page.

item.title: Navigating the AI-Powered Era: Why Modern Deployment Platforms Must Evolve Beyond Jenkins & ArgoCD
item.author: Abhinav Dubey

item.title: Spotlight on SIG Apps
item.content:encoded: In our ongoing SIG Spotlight series, we dive into the heart of the Kubernetes project by talking to the leaders of its various Special Interest Groups (SIGs). This time, we focus on , the group responsible for everything related to developing, deploying, and operating applications on Kubernetes. Sandipan Panda (DevZero) had the opportunity to interview Maciej Szulik (Defense Unicorns) and Janet Kuo (Google), the chairs and tech leads of SIG Apps. They shared their experiences, challenges, and visions for the future of application management within the Kubernetes ecosystem.Sandipan: Hello, could you start by telling us a bit about yourself, your role, and your journey within the Kubernetes community that led to your current roles in SIG Apps?: Hey, my name is Maciej, and I’m one of the leads for SIG Apps. Aside from this role, you can also find me helping SIG CLI and also being one of the Steering Committee members. I’ve been contributing to Kubernetes since late 2014 in various areas, including controllers, apiserver, and kubectl.: Certainly! I'm Janet, a Staff Software Engineer at Google, and I've been deeply involved with the Kubernetes project since its early days, even before the 1.0 launch in 2015. It's been an amazing journey!My current role within the Kubernetes community is one of the chairs and tech leads of SIG Apps. My journey with SIG Apps started organically. I started with building the Deployment API and adding rolling update functionalities. I naturally gravitated towards SIG Apps and became increasingly involved. Over time, I took on more responsibilities, culminating in my current leadership roles.All following answers were jointly provided by Maciej and Janet.Sandipan: For those unfamiliar, could you provide an overview of SIG Apps' mission and objectives? What key problems does it aim to solve within the Kubernetes ecosystem?As described in our charter, we cover a broad area related to developing, deploying, and operating applications on Kubernetes. That, in short, means we’re open to each and everyone showing up at our bi-weekly meetings and discussing the ups and downs of writing and deploying various applications on Kubernetes.Sandipan: What are some of the most significant projects or initiatives currently being undertaken by SIG Apps?At this point in time, the main factors driving the development of our controllers are the challenges coming from running various AI-related workloads. It’s worth giving credit here to two working groups we’ve sponsored over the past years:Best practices and challengesSandipan: SIG Apps plays a crucial role in developing application management best practices for Kubernetes. Can you share some of these best practices and how they help improve application lifecycle management?Implementing health checks and readiness probes ensures that your applications are healthy and ready to serve traffic, leading to improved reliability and uptime. The above, combined with comprehensive logging, monitoring, and tracing solutions, will provide insights into your application's behavior, enabling you to identify and resolve issues quickly.Auto-scale your application based on resource utilization or custom metrics, optimizing resource usage and ensuring your application can handle varying loads.Use Deployment for stateless applications, StatefulSet for stateful applications, Job and CronJob for batch workloads, and DaemonSet for running a daemon on each node. Use Operators and CRDs to extend the Kubernetes API to automate the deployment, management, and lifecycle of complex applications, making them easier to operate and reducing manual intervention.Sandipan: What are some of the common challenges SIG Apps faces, and how do you address them?The biggest challenge we’re facing all the time is the need to reject a lot of features, ideas, and improvements. This requires a lot of discipline and patience to be able to explain the reasons behind those decisions.Sandipan: How has the evolution of Kubernetes influenced the work of SIG Apps? Are there any recent changes or upcoming features in Kubernetes that you find particularly relevant or beneficial for SIG Apps?The main benefit for both us and the whole community around SIG Apps is the ability to extend kubernetes with Custom Resource Definitions and the fact that users can build their own custom controllers leveraging the built-in ones to achieve whatever sophisticated use cases they might have and we, as the core maintainers, haven’t considered or weren’t able to efficiently resolve inside Kubernetes.Sandipan: What opportunities are available for new contributors who want to get involved with SIG Apps, and what advice would you give them?We get the question, "What good first issue might you recommend we start with?" a lot :-) But unfortunately, there’s no easy answer to it. We always tell everyone that the best option to start contributing to core controllers is to find one you are willing to spend some time with. Read through the code, then try running unit tests and integration tests focusing on that controller. Once you grasp the general idea, try breaking it and the tests again to verify your breakage. Once you start feeling confident you understand that particular controller, you may want to search through open issues affecting that controller and either provide suggestions, explaining the problem users have, or maybe attempt your first fix.Like we said, there are no shortcuts on that road; you need to spend the time with the codebase to understand all the edge cases we’ve slowly built up to get to the point where we are. Once you’re successful with one controller, you’ll need to repeat that same process with others all over again.Sandipan: How does SIG Apps gather feedback from the community, and how is this feedback integrated into your work?We always encourage everyone to show up and present their problems and solutions during our bi-weekly meetings. As long as you’re solving an interesting problem on top of Kubernetes and you can provide valuable feedback about any of the core controllers, we’re always happy to hear from everyone.Sandipan: Looking ahead, what are the key focus areas or upcoming trends in application management within Kubernetes that SIG Apps is excited about? How is the SIG adapting to these trends?Definitely the current AI hype is the major driving factor; as mentioned above, we have two working groups, each covering a different aspect of it.Sandipan: What are some of your favorite things about this SIG?Without a doubt, the people that participate in our meetings and on Slack, who tirelessly help triage issues, pull requests and invest a lot of their time (very frequently their private time) into making kubernetes great!SIG Apps is an essential part of the Kubernetes community, helping to shape how applications are deployed and managed at scale. From its work on improving Kubernetes' workload APIs to driving innovation in AI/ML application management, SIG Apps is continually adapting to meet the needs of modern application developers and operators. Whether you’re a new contributor or an experienced developer, there’s always an opportunity to get involved and make an impact.If you’re interested in learning more or contributing to SIG Apps, be sure to check out their SIG README and join their bi-weekly meetings.

item.title: Announcing the end of support for Node.js 14.x and 16.x in AWS CDK
item.author: Adam Keller
item.content:encoded: On May 30th, 2025, the AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) will no longer support Node.js 14.x and 16.x, which reached end of life on 4/30/2023 (14.x) and 9/11/2023 (16.x). This change applies to all AWS CDK components that depend on Node.js, including the AWS CDK CLI, the Construct Library, and broader CDK ecosystem projects such as JSII, Projen, and CDK8s.Node.js 14x and 16.x are past their End of Life and are no longer supported by the Node.js community. This means that there have not been any bug fixes or security updates to these versions. To make sure that we are providing up-to-date and secure libraries, we will drop support for these versions.After , the AWS CDK will no longer support Node.js 14.x and 16.x. While your existing deployments may continue to work, we will not address issues specific to these versions. Any bug reports or support cases that stem from using Node.js 14.x or 16.x will require reproducing the issue on a supported version of Node.js (18.x, 20.x, 22.x – as of February 26th, 2025) before further assistance can be provided.New features for the AWS CDK may rely on APIs or functionalities only available in supported versions of Node.js.Critical security patches and fixes related to Node.js 14.x or 16.x will not be backported.Compatibility testing will no longer be performed for Node.js 16.x, making it difficult to guarantee the CDK’s behavior with that runtime.March 11, 2025 through May 30, 2025The AWS CDK is officially dropping support for Node.js 14.x and 16.x.Any bug fixes or security patches will target only supported versions of Node.js (18.x, 20.x, 22.x – as of March 11th, 2025)Version validation and update stepsCheck your current Node.js versionRun node -v in your environment or CI/CD pipelines to see which version of Node.js you’re currently using.Install or switch your runtime to Node.js using a supported version via a version manager (e.g., nvm) or by downloading an official installer from nodejs.org.Validate your AWS CDK projectsEnsure your deployment scripts, and any third-party dependencies work correctly under a supported version. Test thoroughly in non-production environments.For more information on our deprecation strategy moving forward, please see this RFC, which provides more details.This deprecation is part of our ongoing commitment to provide a secure, high-quality experience for AWS CDK users. By moving to a Node.js Active Long Term Support (LTS) version, you’ll benefit from improved performance, ongoing security patches, and continued AWS CDK innovations. If you have any questions or concerns about this deprecation, please reach out and open an issue in our GitHub repo.

item.title: Kubernetes Secrets vs ConfigMaps — A Detailed Guide
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes Secrets vs ConfigMaps — A Detailed GuideIn Kubernetes, and are resources used to manage external configuration and sensitive data separately from application code. This separation improves security, flexibility, and maintainability by ensuring that applications can be configured dynamically without modifying container images. store non-sensitive data such as application settings, environment variables, and configuration files. store sensitive data like database credentials, API tokens, and SSH keys securely.This document covers what they are, where and why they are used, the risks of storing credentials in ConfigMaps, how Kubernetes stores Secrets, and how to use them in Deployments.A is a Kubernetes object used to store configuration data . It helps keep environment-specific settings separate from application code, ensuring better portability.Key Features of ConfigMap:* Stores configuration data.* Can be used as environment variables, volume mounts, or command-line arguments.* Allows applications to be configured dynamically without requiring redeployment.* Supports storing key-value pairs, YAML, or JSON data.Example ConfigMap Definition (YAML)apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap name: app-config APP_ENV: "production" DB_HOST: "postgres-service"A is a Kubernetes resource designed to store and manage securely. Unlike ConfigMaps, Secrets are and can be encrypted using Kubernetes .* Used for storing such as passwords, API keys, and certificates.* Encoded in to prevent accidental exposure.* Supports using Kubernetes Key Management Service (KMS).* Access is restricted to authorized Pods only.Example Secret Definition (YAML)apiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: db-secretdata: DB_USERNAME: dXNlcm5hbWU= # "username" in base64 DB_PASSWORD: cGFzc3dvcmQ= # "password" in base64Where are ConfigMaps and Secrets Used?Both are used for configuring applications dynamically. They are commonly used in:Why Do We Need to Use ConfigMaps and Secrets?Using provides the following benefits: — Configuration and sensitive data are managed independently of application code. — Sensitive credentials are stored securely in Secrets rather than hardcoding them in application code. — Applications can be updated or modifying container images. — ConfigMaps and Secrets allow easy migration between different environments.What Happens If Credentials Are Used in ConfigMaps?Using credentials in ConfigMaps is a bad security practice because:* ConfigMaps are stored as plain text in etcd (Kubernetes’ database).* Any user with access to the cluster can read the credentials.* Logs and debugging outputs can expose credentials.* No encryption or access control is applied by default.Example of Storing Credentials in a ConfigMap (BAD PRACTICE)apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap name: insecure-config DB_PASSWORD: "my-secret-password"* Instead, use to store sensitive data.How Are Credentials Stored in Kubernetes Secrets?Kubernetes Secrets store data but are .Steps for Encoding SecretsConvert a string to base64:echo -n "my-password" | base64# Output: bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=Store it in a Secret YAML:apiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: secure-secretdata: password: bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=echo "bXktcGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 --decode# Output: my-password: Enable for Secrets in Kubernetes to ensure .How to Use ConfigMaps and Secrets in a Deployment?ConfigMaps and Secrets can be injected into a Pod as:- - Using ConfigMap and Secret as Environment Variables in a DeploymentapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: app replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: my-container env: valueFrom: name: app-config - name: DB_USERNAME secretKeyRef: key: DB_USERNAME valueFrom: name: db-secretMounting ConfigMaps and Secrets as VolumesapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: app1 replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: config-volume name: app-config secret: containers: image: nginx - name: config-volume - name: secret-volumeAccess the data inside the container:kubectl exec -it <app-pod-name> -- cat /etc/config/APP_ENVkubectl exec -it <app1-pod-name> -- cat /etc/secret/DB_PASSWORDSummary of Best Practices* Use for storing credentials instead of ConfigMaps.* Enable for Secrets for .* Use RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) to restrict access to Secrets. * Use Immutable ConfigMaps and Secrets to prevent accidental changes.* commit Secrets or ConfigMaps in Git repositories.

item.title: Introduction to StorageClass and PVC in Kubernetes
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Persistent Volumes (PV) Persistent Volume Claims (PVC) are core components of Kubernetes designed to manage storage needs for applications running in a containerized environment. They ensure that data remains available and persistent, even if the underlying containers or pods are restarted, scaled, or replaced.What is this?Persistent Volume (PV): A Persistent Volume is a storage resource in a Kubernetes cluster that provides a way to store data independently of the lifecycle of pods. It can be backed by various storage types, such as local disks, cloud storage (e.g., AWS EBS, Google Persistent Disk), or network file systems (NFS).Persistent Volume Claim (PVC): A Persistent Volume Claim is a request made by an application for storage. It specifies the amount of storage needed and the access mode (e.g., read/write). The PVC is then matched to an available PV, enabling the application to use the requested storage.: A blueprint for dynamic PV provisioning. Defines and storage is provisioned (e.g., gp3 volumes on AWS). StorageClasses automate PV creation when a PVC is submitted.PersistentVolumeClaims are requests for storage resources by users or applications. They abstract the details of storage implementation from the users, allowing them to request storage without knowing the underlying infrastructure. StorageClasses, on the other hand, define different types of storage offered in a cluster, enabling dynamic provisioning of Persistent Volumes (PVs)Dynamic Provisioning: When a PVC requests storage with a specific StorageClass, Kubernetes can automatically create a PV that matches the requirements3.Storage Types: StorageClasses allow administrators to offer various storage options (e.g., SSDs, HDDs) optimized for different workloads1.Access Modes: PVCs can specify how the storage should be accessed (e.g., ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany)1.Your examples are accurate. Here’s an expanded view with additional scenarios:Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB)Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ)Content Management Systems (WordPress, Drupal)Example: An e-commerce platform uses a PVC with a high-performance StorageClass for its product database, ensuring fast read/write operations even during traffic spikes2.Big data processing (Hadoop, Spark)Machine Learning model storageExample: A data science team uses PVCs with a StorageClass optimized for large file storage to persist machine learning models and datasets4.3. Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD):Example: A CI/CD pipeline uses PVCs to store build artifacts and caches, improving build times and ensuring consistency across different build environments5.Example: A video streaming service uses PVCs with a StorageClass that provides high throughput for storing and processing large media files2.5. Logging and Monitoring:Time-series databases for metricsExample: An organization uses PVCs with a StorageClass that supports high write throughput for storing application logs and monitoring data from multiple microservices5.6. Backup and Disaster Recovery:Example: A financial institution uses PVCs with a StorageClass that supports snapshots for point-in-time recovery of critical financial data4.By leveraging PVCs and StorageClasses, organizations can ensure data persistence, optimize storage performance for specific workloads, and simplify storage management in Kubernetes environments. This approach allows for better resource utilization, improved application reliability, and easier scaling of storage resources as needs changeData Persistence: Ensures that critical application data remains intact even if containers are restarted, replaced, or scaled.Separation of Storage from Pods: Decouples storage from the ephemeral nature of containers, enabling better resource management.Flexibility: Supports various backend storage solutions (local disks, cloud-based storage, network-attached storage).Scalability: Dynamically provisions storage as application needs grow.Reliability: Provides a stable mechanism for managing stateful workloads in Kubernetes.Improved User Experience: Ensures seamless operation for end-users by preventing data loss during disruptions caused by traffic surges, resource limitations, or dependency failures.By using PV and SC in Kubernetes, organizations can build robust and reliable systems capable of handling real-world challenges while ensuring data integrity and availability at all times.To prepare AWS and EKS to use Amazon EBS volumes with the EBS CSI Driver and configure IAM roles using IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), follow these steps:Step 1: Add the EBS CSI Driver Add-on to Your EKS ClusterThe Amazon EBS CSI Driver allows Kubernetes workloads running on EKS to use Amazon EBS volumes as persistent storage.Ensure your EKS cluster is running Kubernetes version 1.20 or later.Install the required tools: eksctl, kubectl, and the AWS CLI.Step 1: Add the EBS CSI Driver Add-onYou can add the driver using eksctl or the AWS Management Console.We are installing the Amazon EBS CSI Driver add-on in our Amazon EKS cluster. This add-on enables Kubernetes to dynamically provision and manage Amazon EBS volumes as Persistent Volumes (PVs) for containerized workloads. By installing the add-on, we allow Kubernetes to interact with Amazon EBS storage resources seamlessly.The commands provided (eksctl and AWS CLI) configure the EBS CSI driver and associate it with an IAM role that grants the necessary permissions to manage EBS volumes.Why Are We Adding the Add-on?The EBS CSI Driver add-on is required because Kubernetes does not natively support direct integration with Amazon EBS. The CSI (Container Storage Interface) driver acts as a bridge between Kubernetes and Amazon EBS, enabling Kubernetes to perform storage operations like:Dynamic Volume Provisioning: Automatically create and attach EBS volumes when a Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) is submitted.Volume Attachment/Detachment: Attach or detach EBS volumes to/from pods as needed.Volume Deletion: Automatically delete unused volumes if configured.Storage Management: Handle advanced features like volume resizing and encryption.Without this add-on, Kubernetes cannot interact with Amazon EBS, and you would need to manually provision and manage storage outside of Kubernetes, which is inefficient and error-prone.eksctl create addon \ --name aws-ebs-csi-driver \ --cluster <your-cluster-name> \ --service-account-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \aws eks create-addon \ --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \ --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \ --service-account-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRoleStep 2: Enable IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)Enabling IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) is a critical step in securely managing access to AWS resources from applications running in Kubernetes pods.In this step, we are enabling IRSA by associating an OIDC (OpenID Connect) Identity Provider with our Amazon EKS cluster. This allows Kubernetes service accounts to assume specific IAM roles, granting pods fine-grained access to AWS resources like Amazon EBS, S3, DynamoDB, or others.Create an OIDC Identity ProviderVerify if your cluster has an OIDC provider:aws eks describe-cluster --name <your-cluster-name> --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text2. If no OIDC provider exists, create one using eksctl:eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \ --region <region> \ --cluster <your-cluster-name> \ --approveStep 3: Create an IAM Role and Policy for the EBS CSI DriverIn this step, we are creating an IAM Policy and an IAM Role to grant the EBS CSI Driver the necessary permissions to manage Amazon EBS volumes. Additionally, we configure a trust relationship to allow the Kubernetes service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) in your EKS cluster to assume this role securely.This policy defines the specific actions that the EBS CSI driver is allowed to perform on AWS resources (e.g., creating, attaching, detaching, and deleting EBS volumes).The policy is attached to an IAM role that will be assumed by the Kubernetes service account used by the EBS CSI driver.2. Creating an IAM Role with a Trust Relationship:The IAM role includes a trust relationship that allows your cluster’s OIDC provider (via IRSA) to assume the role on behalf of the Kubernetes service account.This ensures that only authenticated requests from your cluster can use this role.1. Granting Permissions for EBS ManagementThe EBS CSI driver is responsible for managing Amazon EBS volumes in your Kubernetes cluster. It needs permissions to:Dynamically provision new EBS volumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is created.Attach and detach volumes to/from EC2 instances hosting Kubernetes pods.Delete unused volumes when they are no longer needed (if configured with a Delete reclaim policy).Describe existing volumes and EC2 instances for monitoring and operational purposes.Without these permissions, the EBS CSI driver cannot function, and Kubernetes workloads requiring persistent storage will fail.2. Enabling Secure Authentication via IRSABy using IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), we:Avoid granting broad permissions at the node level (via instance profiles), which could be misused by other pods running on the same node.Scope permissions specifically to the service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) used by the EBS CSI driver.Use short-lived tokens issued by the OIDC provider for authentication, improving security.3. Following Principle of Least PrivilegeThe custom policy ensures that only the required actions are allowed. For example:ec2:CreateVolume allows provisioning of new storage.ec2:AttachVolume and ec2:DetachVolume handle mounting/unmounting of storage to/from pods.ec2:DescribeVolumes and ec2:DescribeInstances are needed for operational visibility.This minimizes risks by ensuring that no unnecessary permissions are granted.4. Enabling Dynamic Storage ManagementThe IAM role enables Kubernetes workloads to dynamically provision and manage persistent storage using Amazon EBS without requiring manual intervention or static credentials.Step 1: Create an IAM PolicyThe EBS CSI Driver requires specific permissions to manage EBS volumes. Use the AWS-managed policy AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy or create a custom policy.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "ec2:AttachVolume", "ec2:DetachVolume", "ec2:DescribeInstances" "Resource": "*" ]Save the above JSON file as ebs-csi-policy.json and create it using:aws iam create-policy \ --policy-name AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \ --policy-document file://ebs-csi-policy.jsonStep 2: Create an IAM RoleThe IAM role allows the Kubernetes service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) to assume it via IRSA. This role is scoped specifically for managing Amazon EBS resources.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Principal": { "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:oidc-provider/<oidc-provider-url>" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity", "Condition": { "<oidc-provider-url>:sub": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa" } ]Save the trust relationship JSON as trust-relationship.json and create it using:aws iam create-role \ --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \ --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-relationship.jsonStep 3: Attach the Policy to the RoleAttach the previously created policy (AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy) to this role:aws iam attach-role-policy \ --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \ --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:policy/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicyGrants only the required permissions for managing Amazon EBS resources.Allows authenticated requests from your cluster’s OIDC provider to assume this role securely.3. Configured a Trust Relationship:Ensures that only authenticated service accounts in your cluster can use this IAM role, tying it specifically to ebs-csi-controller-sa.Linked the custom policy (AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy) with this role so that it has all necessary permissions.Step 4: Associate the IAM Role with Pod IdentityUse the AWS CLI to associate the IAM role with the service account used by the EBS CSI driver (ebs-csi-controller-sa) via Pod Identity Associations.aws eks update-addon \ --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \ --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \ --pod-identity-associations 'serviceAccount=ebs-csi-controller-sa,namespace=kube-system,roleArn=arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole'Replace <your-cluster-name> with your EKS cluster name.Replace <account-id> with your AWS account ID.Replace AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole with the name of your IAM role.This command updates the aws-ebs-csi-driver add-on and associates its service account (ebs-csi-controller-sa) in the kube-system namespace with your IAM role.Step 5: Validate Pod Identity AssociationsOnce you’ve updated the add-on, verify that the Pod Identity Association has been created successfully.aws eks list-pod-identity-associations --cluster-name <your-cluster-name>You should see an entry like this[ { "namespace": "kube-system", "serviceAccount": "ebs-csi-controller-sa", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole" }This confirms that the service account is now associated with the specified IAM role using Pod Identity Associations.Step 5: Validate InstallationCheck that the EBS CSI Driver pods are running:kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep ebs2. Verify the storage classes available in your cluster:Step 6: Create StorageClassA StorageClass in Kubernetes defines how storage resources (like Amazon EBS volumes) are provisioned dynamically. By creating a StorageClass, we are enabling dynamic provisioning of storage for workloads that request PersistentVolumes (PVs). This eliminates the need to manually create and manage storage resources.In this specific case, we are using the Amazon EBS CSI driver (ebs.csi.aws.com) as the provisioner to dynamically manage EBS volumes in AWS.Why We Selected This Configuration1. Dynamic Volume ProvisioningThe StorageClass allows Kubernetes to automatically provision Amazon EBS volumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is created.This simplifies storage management, as you don’t need to pre-create EBS volumes manually in AWS.It ensures that storage is provisioned only when required by workloads.2. Integration with Amazon EBS CSI DriverThe provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com specifies that the Amazon EBS CSI driver will handle the provisioning and management of EBS volumes.The EBS CSI driver is optimized for Kubernetes and supports advanced features like volume resizing, encryption, and snapshots.3. Efficient Volume Binding with WaitForFirstConsumervolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer delays the provisioning and binding of the volume until a pod using the PVC is scheduled.Ensures that the volume is created in the same availability zone as the pod, avoiding cross-zone data transfer costs and improving performance.Useful in multi-AZ clusters where pods can be scheduled in different zones.A user or application creates a PVC specifying:The amount of storage required (e.g., 5Gi).The access mode (e.g., ReadWriteOnce).The StorageClass name (ebs-sc).2. Kubernetes uses the StorageClass (ebs-sc) to dynamically create an Amazon EBS volume using the parameters defined in the StorageClass.3. The Amazon EBS CSI driver provisions the volume in AWS and binds it to the PVC.4. The PVC is then mounted to a pod, allowing applications to store and retrieve data from the volume.Role of Pod Identity AssociationsSince you have already configured Pod Identity Associations using:aws eks update-addon \ --cluster-name <your-cluster-name> \ --addon-name aws-ebs-csi-driver \ --pod-identity-associations 'serviceAccount=ebs-csi-controller-sa,namespace=kube-system,roleArn=arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole'The Amazon EBS CSI driver uses the IAM role associated with ebs-csi-controller-sa to interact with AWS services securely:Create/Delete/Attach/Detach EBS volumes.Ensure proper permissions without relying on node-level instance profiles.apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1kind: StorageClass name: ebs-scprovisioner: ebs.csi.aws.comvolumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer type: gp3Apply the above SC, here is the command to apply and check:kubectl apply -f sc.yamlStep 7: Create a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a request for storage from a StorageClass in Kubernetes. It acts as an abstraction layer between the application and the underlying storage, enabling dynamic provisioning of storage resources like Amazon EBS volumes.In this step, we are creating a PVC that requests storage from the ebs-sc StorageClass (created earlier). This PVC specifies:The amount of storage needed (e.g., 5Gi).The access mode (e.g., ReadWriteOnce).The StorageClass to use (e.g., ebs-sc).apiVersion: v1kind: PersistentVolumeClaim name: ebs-pvc accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce # Only one pod can access at a time resources: storage: 5Gi # Request 5GB of storage storageClassName: ebs-sc # Use the StorageClass created earlierApply the above PvC, here I am apply the pvc in test ns:kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml -n test Dynamic Volume Provisioning:The PVC triggers the dynamic provisioning of an Amazon EBS volume using the EBS CSI driver and the specified StorageClass.Kubernetes automatically creates a PersistentVolume (PV) backed by an EBS volume in AWS.2. Abstract Storage Management:Applications interact with the PVC rather than directly managing the underlying storage infrastructure.This abstraction simplifies storage management and improves portability.3. Specify Storage Requirements:The PVC allows you to define how much storage your application needs (5Gi in this case) and how it will be accessed (ReadWriteOnce).4. Enable Stateful Workloads:Many applications, such as databases, require persistent storage to retain data even if the pod is restarted or rescheduled. The PVC ensures that data persists across pod lifecycles.Once the PVC is created and bound to a dynamically provisioned EBS volume, we deploy a pod that uses this PVC to verify that everything is working correctly.apiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: ebs-test-pod containers: image: busybox # Lightweight image for testing purposes - sleep # Keep the pod running for testing purposes. volumeMounts: - mountPath: "/data" # Mount point inside the container. name: ebs-volume # Link to volume below. - name: ebs-volume # Volume linked to PVC. claimName: ebs-pvc # Reference to PVC created earlierkubectl apply -f pod.yaml -n test Pod-Volume Binding:The pod mounts the dynamically provisioned EBS volume at /data. This verifies that the PVC is correctly bound to a PV and that the volume is accessible from within the pod.Dynamic Provisioning:Check AWS EC2 > Volumes to confirm that an EBS volume has been automatically created and attached to the worker node where the pod is running.Data Persistence:Write some data into /data inside the pod, then delete and recreate the pod. Verify that the data is still present, confirming that it persists across pod restarts.Testing the Setup: Restarting the Pod to Verify PVC ReattachmentIn this test, we will restart the pod using the existing PVC and verify that it reattaches to the same PersistentVolume (backed by Amazon EBS). This simulates a real-world scenario where a pod is restarted (e.g., due to resource constraints or updates) without deleting and recreating it.We will restart the pod (ebs-test-pod) that is already using the PVC (ebs-pvc) to check if it reattaches to the same volume.kubectl rollout restart pod ebs-test-pod -n test # check pod is up #Pvc will be nounded to the pod The pod will stop and start again.Kubernetes will reattach the same EBS volume (provisioned by ebs-pvc) to the pod.kubectl exec -it ebs-test-pod -- sh2. Write Data into testfile.txt:Inside the pod, run:echo "Testing EBS Volume Persistence" > /data/testfile.txt3. Read and Verify the Data:To check the file:Run this command to check if the pod has restarted and is running:kubectl get podsNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEebs-test-pod 1/1 Running 1 <time>The RESTARTS column should show 1, indicating that the pod has been restarted.The STATUS should be Running.3. Verify Data Persistence Inside the PodAccess the restarted pod and verify that it can still access data stored on the EBS volume:kubectl exec -it ebs-test-pod -- sh# Check if previously written data is still availablecat /data/testfile.txtTesting EBS Volume PersistenceThis confirms that data written to the EBS volume before restarting the pod is still accessible.What Happens During a Pod Restart?When you restart a pod, Kubernetes stops and starts it on the same node (if possible).During this process, Kubernetes ensures that any PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) used by the pod remains bound to its PersistentVolume (PV).2. EBS Volume Reattachment:The Amazon EBS CSI driver ensures that the underlying EBS volume remains attached to the worker node hosting the pod.Since we are not deleting or recreating the PVC, no new volume is provisioned.The data stored on the EBS volume persists across restarts because it is external to the pod’s ephemeral storage.

item.title: NGINX Ingress Controller Setup for Kubernetes Networking
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: The is a powerful tool for managing external HTTP/HTTPS traffic to Kubernetes workloads. It simplifies routing, enables SSL termination, and provides advanced configurations for handling complex application networking requirements. This guide provides a to setting up NGINX Ingress for routing traffic to multiple applications in an . It also includes and to ensure a .Why Use NGINX Ingress Controller?: Supports path-based and host-based routing.: High throughput with minimal latency.: Advanced configurations for SSL, rate limiting, and connection handling.: Extensive documentation and community contributions.: Handles large traffic loads with ease.: Supports custom annotations for advanced use cases.: Works seamlessly across multiple cloud providers.: SSL/TLS termination and security headers.: Requires understanding of networking and Kubernetes concepts.: Managing additional resources (like LoadBalancer) increases operational cost.: Configuration can be challenging for beginners.SolutionProsConsLightweight, dynamic configuration.Lacks advanced customizations compared to NGINX.High performance, minimal footprint.Steeper learning curve for advanced use cases.Deep integration with AWS services.Limited portability across cloud providers.Advanced traffic management, observability, and security.High complexity and resource overhead.Why Istio is a Competitor?Istio is a that provides advanced traffic management, observability, and security for microservices. While it’s not a traditional Ingress Controller, it can be used to manage ingress traffic in Kubernetes.Advanced Traffic Management:Supports , , and .Provides fine-grained control over traffic routing.Built-in support for , , and .Integrates with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger.Provides for secure communication between services.Supports role-based access control (RBAC)Supports custom for advanced use cases.Requires a deep understanding of .Steeper learning curve compared to NGINX.Adds significant resource overhead due to the .Managing Istio can be more expensive in terms of time and resources.When to Use Istio Instead of NGINX?If you need advanced traffic management (e.g., canary deployments, traffic splitting).If you require (e.g., metrics, tracing) across your microservices.If you need for secure communication between services.When to Use NGINX Instead of Istio?If you need a solution for ingress traffic.If you want to avoid the and of a service mesh.If you only need and .Before starting, ensure the following prerequisites are met:An with worker nodes.helm: Package manager for Kubernetes.AWS CLI: For managing AWS resources.Ensure proper IAM roles for tagging subnets and provisioning resources.Recommended for production environments to handle routing via DNS.Verify Kubernetes Cluster Access:Kubernetes control plane is running at https://<cluster-endpoint> CoreDNS is running at https://<cluster-endpoint>/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/services/kube-dns:dns/proxy2. Check Existing Subnets and VPC Details:: Ensure subnets are properly tagged for LoadBalancer usage.:kubectl version - client helm version: Ensure compatibility with the Kubernetes cluster.Ensure Nodes Have Sufficient Capacity:: Ensure there’s enough capacity to deploy additional workloads.Step 1: Install NGINX Ingress ControllerWe are installing the NGINX Ingress Controller using Helm, a package manager for Kubernetes. This step ensures that the Ingress Controller is deployed with , , and for production-grade workloads.The default installation may not meet production requirements.Custom configurations ensure the Ingress Controller can handle , , and .helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx helm repo update: This adds the official NGINX Ingress Helm repository and updates the local Helm repository cache.: Helm repositories are like app stores for Kubernetes applications. Adding the repository allows us to install the NGINX Ingress Controller.2. Install the NGINX Ingress Controller:helm install nginx-ingress ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \ - namespace nginx \ - create-namespace \ - set controller.replicaCount=3 \ - set controller.autoscaling.enabled=true \ - set controller.autoscaling.minReplicas=2 \ - set controller.autoscaling.maxReplicas=5 \ - set controller.config.use-forwarded-headers="true" \ - set controller.config.enable-underscores-in-headers="true": This installs the NGINX Ingress Controller with: for high availability. to handle traffic spikes.Custom configurations to support and .: These settings ensure the Ingress Controller is , , and .3. Verify the Installation:kubectl get pods -n nginx: This lists all the Pods in the nginx namespace.nginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-2q4v8 1/1 Running 0 2mnginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-5x6w9 1/1 Running 0 2mnginx-ingress-controller-7c6d8f5b5c-8z7r1 1/1 Running 0 2mnginx-ingress-admission-create-abcde 0/1 Completed 0 2mnginx-ingress-admission-patch-xyzab 0/1 Completed 0 2mThe Pods with names like nginx-ingress-controller-* are the .The Pods with names like nginx-ingress-admission-* are the Admission Controller Jobs (they run once during installation to set up the webhook).Run the following command to list all Services in the nginx namespace:kubectl get svc -n nginxNAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGEnginx-ingress-controller LoadBalancer 10.100.233.226 k8s-nginx-nginxing-bc31958827-2cf0a2893d0e8457.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com 80:31637/TCP,443:30683/TCP 14mnginx-ingress-controller-admission ClusterIP 10.100.211.55Step 2: Deploy Multi-Tenant ApplicationsWe are deploying two applications (codecrypt and mediahive) in separate namespaces (tenant-a and tenant-b). This simulates a where each application is isolated.: Namespaces provide logical separation between applications, ensuring they don’t interfere with each other.: Multi-tenant setups allow you to run multiple applications on the same cluster without conflicts.kubectl create namespace tenant-a kubectl create namespace tenant-b: This creates two namespaces, tenant-a and tenant-b.: Namespaces allow you to isolate resources (e.g., Pods, Services) for different applications.2. Deploy Application 1: CodeCrypt:Create a file named codecrypt.yaml:apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: codecryptspec: selector: app: codecrypt metadata: app: codecrypt containers: image: nginx:1.21 - containerPort: 80apiVersion: v1metadata: namespace: tenant-a selector: ports: targetPort: 80kubectl apply -f codecrypt.yamlThis deploys the codecrypt application with:2 replicas for high availability.A Service to expose the application internally.: This ensures the application is running and accessible within the cluster.3. Deploy Application 2: MediaHive:Create a file named mediahive.yaml:apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: mediahivespec: selector: app: mediahive metadata: app: mediahive containers: image: httpd:2.4 - containerPort: 80apiVersion: v1metadata: namespace: tenant-b selector: ports: targetPort: 80kubectl apply -f mediahive.yamlThis deploys the mediahive application with:2 replicas for high availability.A Service to expose the application internally.This ensures the application is running and accessible within the cluster.Step 3: Configure Advanced Ingress RulesWe are creating an to route traffic to the codecrypt and mediahive applications based on the and . We are also enabling and .: Ingress rules define how external traffic is routed to internal services.: SSL/TLS termination ensures secure communication.: Rate limiting protects backend services from abuse.Create the Ingress Resource:Create a file named ingress.yaml:apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: Ingress name: multi-tenant-ingress annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit: "100r/s" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit-burst: "200" tls: - codecrypt.example.com secretName: tls-secret - host: codecrypt.example.com paths: pathType: Prefix service: port: - host: mediahive.example.com paths: pathType: Prefix service: port:kubectl apply -f ingress.yamlThis creates an Ingress resource that:Routes traffic to codecrypt for codecrypt.example.com.Routes traffic to mediahive for mediahive.example.com.Enables SSL/TLS termination using the tls-secret.Applies rate limiting to protect the backend services.: This ensures secure and efficient traffic routing.Step 4: Enable SSL/TLS TerminationWe are using to automatically provision and manage SSL/TLS certificates for the Ingress Controller.: SSL/TLS termination ensures that traffic between clients and the Ingress Controller is encrypted.: Cert-manager automates certificate provisioning and renewal.kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.11.0/cert-manager.yaml: This installs cert-manager in the cluster.: Cert-manager automates SSL/TLS certificate management.2. Create a ClusterIssuer for Let’s Encrypt:Create a file named cluster-issuer.yaml:apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1kind: ClusterIssuer name: letsencrypt-prod acme: server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory email: admin@example.com name: letsencrypt-prod - http01: class: nginxkubectl apply -f cluster-issuer.yaml: This creates a ClusterIssuer that uses Let’s Encrypt to issue certificates.: This automates SSL/TLS certificate provisioning for the Ingress Controller.Step 5: Validate the SetupWe are testing the setup to ensure everything is working as expected.: Ensures that the Ingress Controller is routing traffic correctly, SSL/TLS is working, and rate limiting is applied.kubectl get ingress -n nginxNAME CLASS HOSTS ADDRESS PORTS AGEmulti-tenant-ingress nginx codecrypt.example.com,mediahive.example.com k8s-nginx-nginxing-bc31958827-2cf0a2893d0e8457.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com 80, 443 5mcurl -I https://codecrypt.example.comcurl -I https://mediahive.example.com: Both requests should return a 200 OK response.curl -H "Host: codecrypt.example.com" <EXTERNAL-IP>: If the rate limit is exceeded, the response should be 429 Too Many Requests.This guide provides a comprehensive walkthrough for setting up the NGINX Ingress Controller in a Kubernetes cluster. By following these steps, you can deploy a ingress solution with , , and . Let me know if you need further assistance!

item.title: Understanding EC2 and EBS: Attach and Mount EBS volume to EC2
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Introduction: Why Do We Need EBS?Elastic Block Store (EBS) is an essential storage solution in AWS that provides high-performance, scalable, and reliable block-level storage for use with EC2 instances. Here are some reasons why EBS is crucial:: Unlike the ephemeral storage provided by instance store volumes, EBS ensures data persistence. Even when an EC2 instance is stopped or terminated, data in the attached EBS volume remains intact.: With multiple volume types, EBS allows fine-tuned configurations for IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second) and throughput, catering to a variety of workloads.: EBS volumes can be resized without downtime, ensuring that your storage grows with your application needs.: EBS supports point-in-time snapshots, enabling quick backup and restore processes.While EBS is a powerful solution, it has some limitations:: EBS volumes are tied to a specific availability zone within a region. They cannot be directly attached across regions.Single Instance Attachment: An EBS volume can only be attached to one EC2 instance at a time (except for Multi-Attach-enabled volumes).: Unlike instance store volumes, EBS incurs additional costs based on storage type, IOPS, and throughput.: High-performance databases requiring low latency and high IOPS.: Applications like Hadoop or Spark needing scalable and high-throughput storage.: Centralized log storage for analytics and monitoring.: Long-term data storage with snapshot capabilities.Step 1: Setting Up EC2 and EBSNavigate to the in the AWS Management Console.Click on and follow these steps:Choose an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) based on your workload.Select an instance type (e.g., t2.micro for basic workloads or m5.large for performance-intensive tasks).Configure instance details such as VPC, subnet, and key pair for SSH access.Add storage for the root volume (e.g., 8 GiB for general usage).Configure security group rules to allow SSH (port 22) and any other necessary ports.Review and launch the instance.Once launched, note down the and (e.g., us-east-1a).Navigate to the section under the menu in the AWS Management Console.Click and configure the following:gp3: General Purpose SSD with customizable IOPS and throughput.io2: Provisioned IOPS SSD for applications needing high IOPS consistency.sc1/st1: Throughput-optimized HDD for large, sequential workloads.: Specify the volume size (e.g., 20 GiB).For gp3, specify the desired IOPS (up to 16,000) and throughput (up to 1,000 MiB/s).For io2, provision up to 64,000 IOPS.: Ensure the volume is in the same AZ as your EC2 instance. (Optional): Create a volume from an existing snapshot if needed.: Add key-value pairs for organization and billing (e.g., Name=MyEBSVolume, Environment=Production).Prerequisites Before Attaching EBSRegion and Availability Zone:The EBS volume must be in the as the EC2 instance. For example, if your instance is in us-east-1a, the EBS volume must also be in us-east-1a.2. Snapshot ID (if applicable):If you’re creating a volume from a snapshot, ensure that the snapshot exists in the same region as the EBS volume.Use tags to manage resources effectively.
