Tags like Name, Environment, and Project help identify and categorize volumes.Device Names for EBS Volumes:When attaching an EBS volume, specify a device name such as /dev/sdb or /dev/sdc. These names represent the logical connection in the instance.Inside the instance, device names may appear as /dev/xvdb, /dev/xvdc, etc., or /dev/nvme0n1 for NVMe-based instances.Use the lsblk command to verify the actual device name in the operating system.Avoid using device names already in use.For consistency, assign device names incrementally (e.g., /dev/sdb, /dev/sdc).Attaching the EBS Volume to the EC2 InstanceNavigate to the section in the AWS Console.Select the EBS volume you created.Click and choose .Select the EC2 instance to attach the volume to.Specify the device name (e.g., /dev/sdb).SSH into the EC2 instance:ssh -i <your-key.pem> ec2-user@<instance-public-ip>Use the lsblk command to confirm the volumes are attachedlsblkNAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTxvda 202:0 0 50G 0 disk /xvdb 202:16 0 20G 0 diskxvdc 202:32 0 30G 0 diskUse the lsblk -o +SERIAL command to cross-check the attached volume's VolumeID:lsblk -o NAME,MAJ:MIN,RM,SIZE,RO,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT,SERIALNAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT SERIALxvda 202:0 0 50G 0 disk / vol-0abcd1234efgh5678xvdb 202:16 0 20G 0 disk vol-0ijkl9012mnop3456The SERIAL column displays the VolumeID of the attached volume, allowing you to cross-verify it with the EBS volume ID in the AWS Console.Checking Disk Usage Before MountingUse the df -h command to display the disk usage:df -h Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on/dev/xvda1 50G 10G 40G 20% /Note that the attached volume does not yet appear in the df -h output since it is not mounted.Step 2: Preparing and Mounting the EBS VolumeCheck if the Disk is EmptyBefore formatting the volume, verify that it does not contain any existing data. Run the following command:Example output for an unformatted disk:If the disk contains data, the output will indicate the filesystem type (e.g., ext4 or xfs).If the disk is empty or its data is no longer needed, proceed to format it. You can use either ext4 or xfs as the filesystem type. Here is how to format the volume:sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdbsudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdbCheck Disk Usage Before MountingBefore mounting the volume, check the current disk usage using:df -hFilesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on/dev/xvda1 50G 1.5G 46G 4% /Mounting the Disk to a Specific DirectoryOnce the EBS volume has been formatted, the next step is to mount it to a specific directory. This allows you to use the storage provided by the EBS volume for a particular application or user. In this section, we’ll explore a scenario where we mount the EBS volume to a new user’s home directory under /home/user1.Scenario: Mounting the Disk to /home/user1Step 1: Verify the User Directory1.Ensure the user directory /home/user1 exists on the instance:2. If the directory does not exist, create it:sudo mkdir -p /home/user13. Set appropriate permissions for the directory:sudo chown user1:user1 /home/user1Replace user1 with the actual username.Step 2: Mount the EBS VolumeMount the EBS volume to the directory:sudo mount /dev/xvdb /home/user1Replace /dev/xvdb with the actual device name of your EBS volume if it differs.2. Verify the mount using the df -h command:Example output: Ensure that /home/user1 is listed as a mount point.Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/xvda1 50G 1.5G 46G 4% / /dev/xvdb 20G 0G 20G 0% /home/user1Step 3: Test the Mount PointCreate a test file in the /home/user1 directory to confirm it is accessible:sudo touch /home/user1/testfile.txt2. Verify the file was created:ls -l /home/user1/testfile.txt3. Check disk usage to confirm the file is stored on the mounted volume:Step 4: Persist the Mount Across RebootsBy default, the mount will not persist across reboots. To make it persistent:Open the /etc/fstab file for editing:2. Add an entry for the EBS volume:/dev/xvdb /home/user1 ext4 defaults,nofail 0 2Replace ext4 with the filesystem type you used when formatting (e.g., xfs).3. Test the fstab configuration:sudo umount /home/user1 sudo mount -aStep 5: Unmounting the Disk (Optional)If you need to unmount the disk, follow these steps:Ensure /home/user1 is no longer listed as a mount point.Step 6: Attaching the Disk and ReassignIf you need reuse the same disk for different user. Like if user1 left Organization, we can reuse the disck to user2First: lets unmount the diskSecond: lets clean the Disk,:sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdbsudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdbOnce this is cleaned, We can mount to new user2. Imagine the user2 has directorysudo mount /dev/xvdb /home/user1Rerun the commands like df -h, lsblk, and Update the fstab with the updated info as this disk is mounted to user2 directoryAlways ensure no files are in use before unmounting a volume. You can use the lsof command to check:If the disk is no longer needed, consider detaching it from the EC2 instance via the AWS Management Console or CLI.With these steps, you’ve successfully mounted an EBS volume to a specific user directory, made the mount persistent, and understood how to manage the mount point effectively.

item.title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore: Enhancing Data Protection with Velero
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: As organizations increasingly adopt cloud-native development practices and Kubernetes, the need for robust data protection strategies becomes paramount. While Kubernetes offers numerous benefits in terms of reliability, scalability, and portability, it also presents unique challenges in data management and protection. Given the highly dynamic nature of Kubernetes environments, where applications and data are constantly changing, frequent and up-to-date backups are essential for disaster recovery, data loss prevention, and mitigation of malicious attacks. However, Kubernetes itself does not provide a built-in backup and restore solution, which creates a significant gap in the data protection strategies of many organizations. This is where Velero steps in.The Challenge of Kubernetes BackupKubernetes environments are inherently complex, and with applications scaling up and down, pods being created and destroyed, and persistent data being frequently updated, ensuring reliable backups becomes a complex task. Traditional backup methods may not be sufficient for cloud-native architectures, as they are often not designed to handle the granular and distributed nature of Kubernetes.Velero offers a tailored solution to this challenge, specifically designed to backup, restore, and migrate Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes. is an open-source tool that simplifies Kubernetes cluster backups and restores. It provides a comprehensive approach to safeguarding both configuration data and persistent volumes in Kubernetes clusters. With Velero, organizations can:Backup entire clusters or specific namespacesRestore clusters after disasters or data lossMigrate cluster resources across different environmentsVelero’s cloud-agnostic design means it can be deployed on any Kubernetes cluster, whether in the cloud or on-premise, making it an ideal solution for multi-cloud strategies.Velero stands out with its powerful features:: Velero allows for granular control over the backup process. You can backup an entire cluster, specific namespaces, or even individual resources using label selectors.: Velero supports automated, recurring backups. This ensures that your Kubernetes cluster is continuously protected with up-to-date backups.: Velero allows for the execution of custom scripts before and after backups. This can be used for tasks like pausing applications, flushing caches, or other pre-backup tasks that enhance data integrity.One of the key differentiators of Velero is its approach to capturing and restoring cluster states. Rather than interacting directly with Kubernetes’ etcd database, Velero interacts with the Kubernetes API to gather resource states. This approach provides several benefits:Flexible Resource Selection: Velero enables users to selectively backup and restore resources based on their needs, which allows for greater control over data protection and recovery.Managed Kubernetes Compatibility: Velero works seamlessly with managed Kubernetes services (like AWS EKS, GKE, AKS), where direct access to etcd is often restricted.: Velero supports resources that are exposed through aggregated API servers, ensuring that custom resources can also be backed up and restored.In addition to backing up Kubernetes configuration data (like Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, etc.), Velero also supports backing up persistent data using either storage platform snapshots or the file-level backup tool . This ensures that data volumes are protected alongside Kubernetes resources.Velero integrates directly into Kubernetes through Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs). These CRDs define backup, restore, and scheduling operations that Velero manages using its controllers. Here’s a look at how Velero operates:: A backup is initiated through the Velero CLI or UI.: The Kubernetes API server creates a object that defines the backup parameters (e.g., what namespaces, resources, or volumes to include).BackupController Processing: The BackupController validates and processes the Backup object, ensuring it meets the criteria for the backup job.: Velero queries the Kubernetes API server to gather the resources specified in the backup request (e.g., Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, PersistentVolumes).: Once collected, the backup data (both configuration and persistent volumes) is uploaded to the specified object storage service (such as AWS S3, GCS, or Azure Blob Storage).: A restore is initiated by creating a object through the Velero CLI or UI.Restore Object Processing: Velero’s RestoreController processes the Restore object, verifying the request and determining which resources to restore.: Velero re-applies the backed-up Kubernetes resources to the cluster by submitting the backed-up YAML manifests to the API server.: If persistent volumes were included in the backup, they are reattached to the appropriate pods.Here are the prerequisites which are required to have velero tested and setup:Ensure you have an active Kubernetes cluster running version 1.28, such as Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) 1.28.The cluster should have sufficient resources (memory, CPU, etc.) to run Velero components and associated workloads.Note: Check the version compatibility and select the specific version required for velero to be installed in EKSIf Upgrading the velero from v1.9.x to > 1.10.x we must run the following commands to install necessary CRD’sCaution: From Velero v1.10, except for using restic to do file-system level backup and restore, kopia is also been integrated, so there would be a little bit of difference when upgrading to v1.10 from a version lower than v1.10.0.Update the Velero custom resource definitions (CRDs) to include schema changes across all CRDs that are at the core of the new features in this release:velero install --crds-only --dry-run -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -NOTE: Since velero v1.10.0 only v1 CRD will be supported during installation, therefore, the v1.10.0 will only work on kubernetes version >= v1.16The (Command Line Interface) must be installed and configured on your local machine or the system where you are setting up Velero.Configure AWS CLI with sufficient IAM permissions to manage the S3 bucket, EBS volumes, and any other AWS resources used by Velero.Velero requires an IAM role or user with the following minimum permissions to back up and restore resources:: For storing and retrieving backup files.: To create snapshots of persistent volumes backed by Amazon EBS.The IAM role should have these policies attached: AmazonS3FullAccess, AmazonEC2FullAccess, AmazonEKSClusterPolicy.Create an S3 bucket where Velero will store backup files. The bucket should be in the same AWS region as your EKS cluster.Ensure that the S3 bucket has the proper permissions and lifecycle policies, if required, for data retention.aws s3 mb s3://<your-velero-backups-bucket> --region <your-region>Install the on your local machine, which is required to interact with the Velero server on your Kubernetes cluster.# MacOSbrew install velerocurl -L https://github.com/vmware-tanzu/velero/releases/download/v1.11.0/velero-v1.11.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz | tar -xzsudo mv velero-v1.11.0-linux-amd64/velero /usr/local/bin/6. Storage Class for SnapshotsTo ensure Velero operates smoothly within your EKS cluster, you’ll need to configure certain AWS services, such as IAM roles and OIDC providers. Here’s a step-by-step guide for setting up the required IAM components and CSI driver for Amazon EBS.6.1 — Step 1: Create an IAM OIDC Provider for Your ClusterVelero requires IAM roles to perform operations like accessing S3 for backups and managing persistent volumes. You can configure an IAM OIDC provider for your EKS cluster as follows:Identify OIDC ID for the Cluster:oidc_id=$(aws eks describe-cluster --name <Your-Cluster-Name> --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text | cut -d '/' -f 5)2. Check for Existing OIDC Providers:aws iam list-open-id-connect-providers | grep $oidc_id3. Associate OIDC Provider with Your Cluster:eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --region=<Your-Region> --cluster=<Your-Cluster-Name> --approve4. Verify OIDC Provider Association:aws iam list-open-id-connect-providers | grep $oidc_idArn: arn:aws:iam::<Account-ID>:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.<Your-Region>.amazonaws.com/id/<Your-OIDC-ID>6.2 — Step 2: Create Amazon EBS CSI Driver IAM RoleVelero uses Amazon EBS CSI drivers to manage persistent volumes in EKS. You’ll need to create an IAM role for the EBS CSI driver and attach it to your EKS cluster.Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using eksctl create iamserviceaccount \ --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \ --namespace kube-system \ --cluster <Your-Cluster-Name> \ --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \ --approve \ --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole#make sure to change the eks cluster--cluster <Your-Cluster-Name>This setup ensures that the EKS cluster is correctly configured to support Velero for backups and persistent volume snapshots.UI : To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI DriverCreate a namespace where Velero will operate, typically velero.kubectl create namespace velero8. Network and Security GroupsVerify that the network and security groups associated with your EKS cluster allow for necessary ingress and egress traffic. Velero requires network connectivity to AWS services, including the S3 bucket and EC2 for snapshot creation.Configure proper inbound and outbound rules for Velero pods, especially if they run in a VPC with restrictive policies.To install NGINX on your EKS 1.28 cluster as a demo for Velero, follow these simple steps:Ensure kubectl is configured to interact with your EKS cluster:aws eks --region us-west-1 update-kubeconfig --name eks1kubectl config current-context#will print the current k8s cluster Create a namespace for the NGINX deployment:kubectl create namespace nginx-demoCreate a NGINX deployment:kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx -n nginxNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEpod/nginx-7854ff8877-z7n5w 1/1 Running 0 18sNAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEdeployment.apps/nginx 1/1 1 1 18sNAME DESIRED CURRENT READY AGEreplicaset.apps/nginx-7854ff8877 1 1 1 18sThese are some of the prerequisites for configuring the Velero in EKS.Velero Backup Restore (EKS1, EKS2)Primary EKS1 Cluster Setup:If you are using cluster config and to access cluster you need IAM User or Role. We might already use creds to access the cluster. So if you want to use same user for Velero we need a policy for backup and restore into S3 bucket. This policy must be attached to IAM user.If you want to separate the IAM Users. If a new user is required only for this Disaster Management we can create a separate users. Here in this doc we are separating the Users.Step 1: Create an S3 BucketLog in to the AWS Management Console. and click .Configure the bucket settings:: velero-eks1-bucket (or any globally unique name).: us-east-1 (or your preferred AWS region).: Block all public access for security.: Enable versioning (recommended for Velero).S3 Access. Run the following command to verify that your credentials and permissions are correctly set up for S3 access:This should list the contents of the bucket, or return an empty result if the bucket is empty. If there’s an access error, review the IAM policy attached to the user or role.aws s3 ls s3://velero-eks-globala --region us-east-1Step 2: Create an IAM User for Velero in the AWS Console and create a new user:: Programmatic access (required for CLI and API). to the user: [Need to update s3 bucket]: This policy will allow Velero to manage backups in the S3 bucket.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSnapshots", "ec2:CreateVolume", "ec2:DeleteSnapshot" "Resource": "*" { "Action": [ "s3:DeleteObject", "s3:AbortMultipartUpload", "s3:ListMultipartUploadParts" ], "arn:aws:s3:::velero-eks-cluster-backup/*" }, "Effect": "Allow", "s3:ListBucket" "Resource": [ "arn:aws:s3:::velero-eks-cluster-backup" ] ]EOF3. Save AccessKey/SecretKey in a file. Steps to Save AWS Credentials in a Different File and Pass Them to Velero:3.1 Create a Separate Credentials File:Save your AWS credentials in a file at a custom path. For example, create a file named velero-creds in your home directory::[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_IDaws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEYStep 3: Create IAM ServiceAccount EKS1Primary Cluster Name: EKS1The allows your EKS cluster to manage (Elastic Block Store) for your applications running on Kubernetes. It provides dynamic provisioning of persistent storage for your workloads.Without this IAM ServiceAccount and associated IAM role, the EBS CSI driver wouldn’t have the necessary permissions to manage EBS volumes in AWS (e.g., creating and attaching volumes).1: Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using This command sets up the that allow the Amazon EBS CSI (Container Storage Interface) Driver to interact with AWS services (like EBS volumes) on behalf of your EKS cluster.eksctl create iamserviceaccount \ --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \ --namespace kube-system \ --cluster eks1 \ --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \ --approve \ --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole#make sure to change the eks cluster--cluster <Your-Cluster-Name>This setup ensures that the EKS cluster is correctly configured to support Velero for backups and persistent volume snapshots.To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI DriverCheck the ServiceAccount in Kube-Systemkubectl get sa ebs-csi-controller-sa -n kube-system -o yamlapiVersion: v1automountServiceAccountToken: truekind: ServiceAccount annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxxxxx:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole_eks1 labels: app.kubernetes.io/component: csi-driver app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: EKS app.kubernetes.io/name: aws-ebs-csi-driver name: ebs-csi-controller-sa2: Install Velero in EKS — Primary:Update the Velero custom resource definitions (CRDs) to include schema changes across all CRDs that are at the core of the new features in this release:velero install --crds-only --dry-run -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -NOTE: Since velero v1.10.0 only v1 CRD will be supported during installation, therefore, the v1.10.0 will only work on kubernetes version >= v1.16Velero IAM user, we have created IAM Policy attached to this user and we have AWS credentials in a file at a custom path.:[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_IDaws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEYRequired Parameters: Velero Version, AWS plugin, Credential FilePath, Bucketvelero install \ --provider aws \ --image velero/velero:v1.14.0 \ --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.10.0 \ --bucket velero-eks-cluster-backup \ --secret-file ~/velero-creds \ --backup-location-config region=us-east-1 \ --snapshot-location-config region=us-east-1 \kubectl get all -n veleroNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEpod/velero-xxxxx-xxxx 1/1 Running 0 26dNAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEdeployment.apps/velero 1/1 1 1 26dFirst, ensure that the nginx namespace exists. If not, create it:kubectl create namespace nginxNow, deploy the NGINX app in the nginx namespace. You can do this using a simple deployment with a service for accessing NGINX:kind: Deployment name: nginx-deploymentspec: selector: app: nginx metadata: app: nginx containers: image: nginx:latest - containerPort: 80Once the deployment and service are running correctly:kubectl get pods -n nginxWe can check the current NSNAME STATUS AGEdefault Active 26dkube-node-lease Active 26dkube-public Active 26dkube-system Active 26dtest Active 25dvelero Active 26dnginx Active 26dVelero uses S3 to store EKS backups when running in AWS. With Velero installed, we will move forward with installing an application in our Primary cluster that we will back up and restore in our Recovery cluster. Customers will be able to follow the steps below to back up and restore their own applications in their own Amazon EKS clusters as well.Create a backup of the Primary cluster. Be sure to switch your kubectl context back to the Primary cluster before running the command below. : When we execute velero backup command, it will make a call to the Kubernetes API server and create a backup of objects like namespaces, storageclass, pv, pvc etc. The backup controller will query to the API server for the objects to be backed up and then makes a call to the S3 bucket where the backup will be saved in tar format. To create periodic backups with Velero by configuring a ‘schedule’.This is to describe the backup and status of the backupHere are the commands to take backup of single ns, backup excluding few ns, wholecluster backup— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —Here are the backup commands can be used for velero# Create a backup every 6 hours with the @every notationvelero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 6h"# Create a daily backup of the namespacevelero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 24h" --include-namespaces <namspacename># Create a weekly backup, each living for 90 days (2160 hours)velero schedule create <SCHEDULE_NAME> --schedule="@every 168h" --ttl 2160h0m0s ##default TTL time is 720h# Create a backup including the test and default namespacesvelero backup create backup --include-namespaces test,default# Create a backup excluding the kube-system and default namespacesvelero backup create backup --exclude-namespaces kube-system,default# To backup entire clustervelero backup create <BACKUPNAME>#To backup namespace in a clustervelero backup create <BACKUPNAME> --include-namespaces <NAMESPACENAME># Daily Backup, 7 days retention policyvelero create schedule wordpress-bkup-daily-ret-7d \--ttl 168h0m0s \--include-namespaces wordpress-ns# Daily Backup, 7 days retention policyvelero schedule create duokey-bkup-daily-ret-7d --schedule="0 0 * * *" --ttl 168h0m0s --selector app=duokey# Weekly Backup, 30 days retention policyvelero schedule create duokey-bkup-weekly-ret-30d --schedule="1 0 */7 * *" --ttl 720h0m0s --selector app=duokey# Monthly Backup, 12 months retention policyvelero schedule create duokey-bkup-monthly-ret-12m --schedule="0 8 1 * *" --ttl 8760h0m0s --selector app=duokey# Yearly Backup, 3 years retention policyvelero schedule create duokey-bkup-yearly-ret-3y --schedule="0 16 1 5 *" --ttl 43800h0m0s --selector app=duokeyCreate Backup — Single NSCreated backup, for Test NS.velero backup create test1 --include-namespaces testvelero backup getNAME STATUS ERRORS WARNINGS CREATED EXPIRES STORAGE LOCATION SELECTORtest1-20220922174845 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT 26m default <none>velero restore logs test1-20220922174845velero backup describe test1Create Backup — wholeclusterCreated backup wholeclustervelero backup create wholecluster2. Check the wholecluster backup status3. Check the backups in S3— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —Backups created in EKS1 for ns test and wholecluster. These backups are pushed to same bucket as velero installed in EKS using the bucket velero-eks-cluster-backupHere is the commands I used to create a schedule for the backup. I want to schedule the backup during off hours. The backup which I schedule must be deleted after 30 min and take backup every 30 min.velero schedule create eks-primary-schedule --schedule="@every 1h" --ttl 0h30m0s velero schedule create eks-primary30m-schedule --schedule="@every 30m" --ttl 0h30m0svelero schedule getNAME STATUS CREATED SCHEDULE BACKUP TTL LAST BACKUP SELECTOReks-primary-schedule Enabled 2022-09-26 12:25:04 -0700 MDT @every 1h 30m0s 6h ago <none>eks-primary30m-schedule Enabled 2022-09-26 21:22:04 -0600 MDT @every 1h 30m0s6h ago <none>velero backup getNAME STATUS ERRORS WARNINGS CREATED EXPIRES STORAGE LOCATION SELECTOReks-primary-schedule-20220927011104 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT 26m default <none>eks-primary30m-schedule-20220927032204 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 21:22:04 -0600 MDT 23m default <none>eks-primary30m-schedule-20220927025204 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 20:52:04 -0600 MDT 6m ago default <none>velero schedule describe eks-primary-scheduleName: eks-primary-scheduleNamespace: veleroAnnotations: <none>Schedule: @every 1h Namespaces: Excluded: <none> Included: * Cluster-scoped: auto Storage Location: Velero-Native Snapshot PVs: auto TTL: 30m0sLast Backup: 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT------------------------------------------------------------------------------------velero schedule describe eks-primary-scheduleName: eks-primary-scheduleLabels: <none>Phase: EnabledBackup Template: Included: * Resources: Excluded: <none> Label selector: <none> Velero-Native Snapshot PVs: auto Hooks: <none>Last Backup: 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —Secondary Cluster Name: EKS21: Create the EBS CSI IAM Role Using eksctl or UI:This command sets up the that allow the Amazon EBS CSI (Container Storage Interface) Driver to interact with AWS services (like EBS volumes) on behalf of your EKS cluster.eksctl create iamserviceaccount \ --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \ --namespace kube-system \ --cluster eks1 \ --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy \ --approve \ --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole#make sure to change the eks cluster--cluster <Your-Cluster-Name>This setup ensures that the EKS cluster is correctly configured to support Velero for backups and persistent volume snapshots.To setup, Go to EKS cluster in AWS. Go to ADD-ONs and add this EBS CSI DriverCheck the ServiceAccount in Kube-Systemkubectl get sa ebs-csi-controller-sa -n kube-system -o yamlapiVersion: v1automountServiceAccountToken: truekind: ServiceAccount annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxxxxx:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole_eks1 labels: app.kubernetes.io/component: csi-driver app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: EKS app.kubernetes.io/name: aws-ebs-csi-driver name: ebs-csi-controller-sa2: Install Velero in EKS — Secondary:Update the Velero custom resource definitions (CRDs) to include schema changes across all CRDs that are at the core of the new features in this release:velero install --crds-only --dry-run -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -NOTE: Since velero v1.10.0 only v1 CRD will be supported during installation, therefore, the v1.10.0 will only work on kubernetes version >= v1.16Velero IAM user, we have created IAM Policy attached to this user and we have AWS credentials in a file at a custom path.:[default]aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_IDaws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY3. Velero Install ScondaryRequired Parameters: Velero Version, AWS plugin, Credential FilePath, Bucketvelero install \ --provider aws \ --image velero/velero:v1.14.0 \ --plugins velero/velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.10.0 \ --bucket velero-eks-cluster-backup \ --secret-file ~/velero-creds \ --backup-location-config region=us-east-1 \ --snapshot-location-config region=us-east-1 \kubectl get all -n veleroNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEpod/velero-xxxxx-xxxx 1/1 Running 0 26dNAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEdeployment.apps/velero 1/1 1 1 26dWe can use use the velero restore createcommand is used to restore resources and data that have been backed up using Velero. Here’s a detailed description of how the restore process works:Velero Restore Commands : create a restore named "restore-1" from backup "backup-1"velero restore create restore-1 --from-backup backup-1# create a restore with a default name ("backup-1-<timestamp>") from backup "backup-1"velero restore create --from-backup backup-1# create a restore from the latest successful backup triggered by schedule "schedule-1"velero restore create --from-schedule schedule-1# create a restore from the latest successful OR partially-failed backup triggered by schedule "schedule-1"velero restore create --from-schedule schedule-1 --allow-partially-failed# create a restore for only persistentvolumeclaims and persistentvolumes within a backupvelero restore create --from-backup backup-2 --include-resources persistentvolumeclaims,persistentvolumesvelero restore create --from-backup <backupname>velero restore create <RESTORE_NAME> --from-schedule <SCHEDULE_NAME># Create a restore including the test and default namespacesvelero restore create --from-backup backup --include-namespaces nginx,default# Create a restore excluding the kube-system and default namespacesvelero restore create --from-backup backup --exclude-namespaces kube-system,defaultvelero restore logs <RESTORE_NAME>How Velero Restore Works:Velero retrieves the backup data stored in your backup storage location (e.g., S3 for AWS).It then restores Kubernetes resources, such as Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, etc., to the specified namespace or the original namespace if not specified.Restoring Persistent Volumes:If the backup includes persistent volume claims (PVCs), Velero restores them along with the associated volumes (e.g., EBS volumes in AWS).Velero may need to recreate volumes and restore data from snapshots if configured.Restoring Configuration and Secrets:Configuration data, secrets, and other Kubernetes objects are restored as they were at the time of the backup.Handling Resource Conflicts:If resources already exist in the cluster with the same name as those being restored, Velero may overwrite them or handle conflicts based on the restore options specified.After initiating the restore, you can check the status of the restore operation to see if it completed successfully or if there were any errors.— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —To restore backups from eks1 to eks2, you need to follow a series of steps to ensure that the resources from eks1 are correctly restored into the eks2 cluster. Here’s a step-by-step guide to document the restore process:: Ensure that Velero is installed and configured in the eks2 cluster.: The backups from eks1 (e.g., test1 and wholecluster) should be available in the backup storage location (e.g., S3 bucket).Steps to Restore Backups in eks2:Ensure that Velero can see the backups from eks1 in the backup storage location.velero backup getNAME STATUS ERRORS WARNINGS CREATED EXPIRES STORAGE LOCATION SELECTORtest1-20220922174845 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT 26m default <none> STATUS ERRORS WARNINGS CREATED EXPIRES STORAGE LOCATION SELECTOReks-primary-schedule-20220927011104 Completed 0 0 2022-09-26 19:11:04 -0600 MDT 26m default <none>kubectl get nsNAME STATUS AGEdefault Active 6h30meksbackupdemo Active 5h20mkube-node-lease Active 6h30mkube-public Active 6h30mkube-system Active 6h30mvelero Active 5h55mRestore thens in EKS2velero restore create --from-backup test1 --namespace testRestore eks-primary30m scheduled backupvelero restore create --from-backup eks-primary30m-schedule-20220928155205Restore request "eks-primary30m-schedule-20220928155205-20220928101709" submitted successfully.Run `velero restore describe eks-primary30m-schedule-20220928155205-20220928101709` or `velero restore logs eks-primary30m-schedule-20220928155205-20220928101709` for more details.kubectl get nsNAME STATUS AGEdefault Active 6h30meksbackupdemo Active 5h20mkube-node-lease Active 6h30mkube-public Active 6h30mkube-system Active 6h30mvelero Active 5h55mtest Active 1d

item.title: Helm3 Basic Understanding
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Helm is a popular package manager for Kubernetes, designed to simplify the deployment and management of applications on Kubernetes clusters. This documentation aims to provide you with a comprehensive guide to using Helm 3 effectively.Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes, the open-source platform used to manage containerized applications. It is often compared to traditional package managers like apt for Debian-based systems or yum for RPM-based systems, but it is specifically designed for Kubernetes.Helm helps you manage Kubernetes applications through Helm charts. A Helm chart is a collection of files that describe a related set of Kubernetes resources. It simplifies the process of defining, installing, and upgrading even the most complex Kubernetes applications.Using Helm brings several benefits, particularly in the context of managing Kubernetes applications:Using Helm brings several benefits, particularly in the context of managing Kubernetes applications:Simplified Application Deployment:Helm charts allow you to define, install, and upgrade Kubernetes applications quickly and easily.With a single Helm command, you can deploy a multi-component application with all its dependencies.Helm allows you to version your Kubernetes applications, making it easy to roll back to a previous version if needed.Each release of a chart is tracked and versioned, ensuring you can manage upgrades and downgrades smoothly.3. Reuse and Share Charts:Helm charts can be shared and reused, promoting collaboration within and between teams.You can find pre-built charts for popular applications in public repositories like Helm Hub.4. Configuration Management:Helm provides a way to manage and customize your application’s configuration using values files and template files.This allows you to manage complex configurations and use the same chart for multiple environments (development, staging, production) with different configurations.:Helm can manage dependencies for your charts. If your application relies on other applications or services, Helm can ensure all dependencies are installed and configured correctly.Helm vs. Traditional Kubernetes YAMLHelm offers several advantages over managing Kubernetes applications using traditional YAML files:Modularity and Reusability:Helm charts promote modularity by allowing you to break down your Kubernetes configurations into smaller, reusable components.Traditional YAML files tend to be monolithic and specific to a particular environment.2. Easier Upgrades and Rollbacks:Helm makes it straightforward to upgrade applications and roll back to previous versions, handling all the complexities of Kubernetes resource management.With traditional YAML files, manual intervention is often required to update or revert changes.3. Consistent and Repeatable Deployments:Helm charts ensure that deployments are consistent and repeatable across different environments.Traditional YAML deployments might vary between environments, leading to potential inconsistencies.4. Parameterization and Templates:Helm charts support parameterization and templating, enabling dynamic configuration based on the deployment environment.Traditional YAML files lack built-in templating and often require manual edits for different configurations.:Helm can manage dependencies between charts, ensuring that all required components are installed and configured.Managing dependencies with traditional YAML files can be cumbersome and error-prone.Helm 3 simplified the architecture by removing Tiller, leading to a more secure and streamlined process. Now, the Helm Client directly interacts with the Kubernetes API server.: The Helm Client remains a command-line tool for managing charts. It directly communicates with the Kubernetes API server to perform operations such as installations, upgrades, and rollbacks.: With Helm 3, the Helm Client interacts directly with the Kubernetes API server, leveraging native Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) for security and access control.Helm consists of several key components:: A Helm chart is a collection of files that describe a Kubernetes application. Charts are packaged as .tgz files and can be shared via repositories.: Helm repositories are collections of packaged charts. They provide a way to distribute charts and make them available for installation.: A release is an instance of a chart running in a Kubernetes cluster. Helm manages the lifecycle of these releases, including installation, upgrade, and rollback.: Templates are files in a chart that contain Kubernetes resource definitions written in YAML, but with placeholders for dynamic values. These placeholders are replaced with actual values when the chart is deployed.Helm Search: Finding ChartsUse helm search to discover available charts in configured repositories. For example:2. Repo Add: Adding Helm RepositoriesAdd new Helm repositories to access charts from different sources. For instance:helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami3. Pull Helm: Downloading ChartsDownload Helm charts without installing them using helm pull. Example:4. Repo Update: Updating RepositoriesUpdate the local cache of Helm repositories to get the latest charts and metadata:5. Show Chart: Viewing Chart DetailsDisplay detailed information about a specific Helm chart, such as its versions and dependencies:helm show chart bitnami/nginx6. Show Values: Viewing Chart ValuesView the default configuration values used by a Helm chart with helm show values:helm show values bitnami/nginx7. Install: Deploying a Helm ChartDeploy a Helm chart onto your Kubernetes cluster using helm install.helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginxDeploy a Helm chart onto your Kubernetes cluster using helm install. You can pass arguments directly during installation using the --set flag to override specific values from the chart's values.yaml.Let’s say you want to install the NGINX chart (bitnami/nginx) and customize some values during installation.helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=3,image.tag=latest -n nginxmy-nginx is the name you give to the Helm release.bitnami/nginx specifies the chart repository name and chart name.--set is used to override specific values defined in the chart's values.yaml.replicaCount=3 sets the number of replicas to 3.image.tag=latest sets the Docker image tag to latest.7.1 Check Helm Release StatusAfter installing, you can use the following commands to check the status and details of your Helm release:: This command lists all installed Helm releases on the Kubernetes cluster.Show Detailed Information about a Release: This command shows detailed information about the my-nginx release, including configuration, status, and Kubernetes resources.helm get all my-nginx -n nginxThis Kubernetes command retrieves the status of pods belonging to the my-nginx release, filtering by the label app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-nginx.8. Generate Helm Manifest YAMLAfter installing a Helm release using helm install, you can use the helm manifest command to generate Kubernetes YAML manifests for that release. Here’s how you can do it:helm manifest my-nginx -n nginx This command renders the templates (templates/) used in the NGINX chart, including Deployment, Service, and other resources, based on the values provided during installation (values.yaml, or overridden with --set flags).The output of helm manifest will be the combined Kubernetes YAML manifests for all resources managed by the my-nginx release, including labels, annotations, and other metadata defined in the Helm chart.Redirecting Output to a File: If you want to save the generated YAML to a file, you can redirect the output using standard shell redirection. This will save the generated Kubernetes manifests into a file named my-nginx-manifest.yaml in your current directory.helm manifest my-nginx > my-nginx-manifest.yaml cat my-nginx-manifest.yaml9. Upgrade the Helm ReleaseThis command upgrades the existing my-nginx release deployed from the bitnami/nginx chart, changing the replicaCount from 3 to 5.helm upgrade my-nginx bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=5 -n nginx10. Uninstall: Removing a Helm ReleaseRemove a Helm release and its associated resources from your Kubernetes cluster with helm uninstall:helm uninstall my-nginx -n nginxTo create a Helm chart, you can use the helm create <chart-name> command. This command generates a directory structure with several predefined files and directories. Let's walk through this command and explain the structure it creates:This command will create a directory named mychart with the following structure:# Chart Structure and Filesnginx/ ├── charts/ │ ├── deployment.yaml ├── Chart.yaml defines metadata about the Helm chart (mychart), including dependencies ( contains configurable values for mychart, including settings for NGINX deployment and MySQL configuration.templates/deployment.yaml is a Kubernetes Deployment template for NGINX, using values from values.yaml to customize the deployment and integrate with dependencies like MySQL.apiVersion: v2name: mychartdescription: A Helm chart for Kubernetestype: applicationappVersion: 1.0.0 - name: mysql repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami: Specifies the API version of the Helm chart (v2 in this case).: The unique name of the Helm chart (mychart in this example).: A brief description of what the Helm chart does.: Indicates that this chart is an application chart.: The version of the Helm chart itself (0.1.0).: The version of the application being deployed by the chart (1.0.0).: Lists other charts (mysql in this case) that this chart depends on. Dependencies ensure that required services are installed along with the main application.The dependencies section specifies that mychart depends on the MySQL chart (bitnami/mysql) version 1.6.7 from the Bitnami repository. This means when you install or upgrade mychart, Helm will also manage the lifecycle of mysql chart, ensuring it is installed, upgraded, or removed as necessary.How Dependencies Are Used in Deploymentname: my-nginxnamespace: default repository: nginx port: 80 mysqlRootPassword: rootpassword mysqlPassword: mypassword mysqlDatabase: mydatabase: Specifies the name of the NGINX deployment (my-nginx).: The Kubernetes namespace where the deployment will be installed (default in this case).: Defines the Docker image details for NGINX (nginx:1.19.2).: Specifies details for the Kubernetes Service associated with NGINX.: Refers to the name of a Kubernetes Secret (my-secret) containing sensitive data.: Contains configuration values specific to MySQL, such as passwords and database names.templates/deployment.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: {{ .Values.name }}-nginx namespace: {{ .Values.namespace }} labels:spec: selector: app: {{ .Values.name }} metadata: app: {{ .Values.name }} containers: - name: {{ .Values.name }}-nginx image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}" - containerPort: {{ .Values.service.port }} - name: MYSQL_HOST value: {{ .Release.Name }}-mysql - name: MYSQL_DATABASE value: {{ .Values.mysql.mysqlDatabase }} - name: MYSQL_USER value: {{ .Values.mysql.mysqlUser }} - name: MYSQL_PASSWORD secretKeyRef: name: {{ .Values.secretName }} key: mysql-passwordThis deployment.yaml template defines a Kubernetes Deployment for NGINX.It uses values from values.yaml (like .Values.name, .Values.namespace, .Values.image.repository, etc.) to configure the deployment.Environment variables (MYSQL_HOST, MYSQL_DATABASE, MYSQL_USER, MYSQL_PASSWORD) are set using values from values.yaml and the Helm release context (Release.Name).MYSQL_HOST is set to the hostname of the MySQL service provided by the dependency ({{ .Release.Name }}-mysql).Installing NGINX using HelmNow, let’s proceed with deploying NGINX using Helm:Initialize Helm (if not already initialized):Navigate to the directory containing your Helm chart (:my-nginx is the release name you assign.. indicates that the current directory (nginx/) contains the Helm chart to be installed.helm install my-nginx . -n nginx:helm uninstall my-nginx -n nginx

item.title: Configuring Grafana with AWS EBS Persistent Storage and Custom Dashboards, Plugins and DataSources…
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Configuring Grafana with AWS EBS Persistent Storage and Custom Dashboards, Plugins and DataSources on Amazon EKSIn this guide, we will configure Grafana on Amazon EKS using Persistent Volume Claims (PVC) and Storage Classes to ensure data persistence, even if the pod restarts. We’ll also create custom dashboards for monitoring.Setting Up Grafana with PVC and Storage ClassGrafana is a powerful tool for monitoring and observability. By using PVCs and Storage Classes, we can store Grafana data on Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store), ensuring that our data remains intact across pod restarts.Persistent storage is essential for applications that require data retention, such as Grafana. Amazon EBS provides reliable and high-performance block storage that integrates seamlessly with EKS.Creating Custom DashboardsGrafana allows you to create custom dashboards tailored to your specific monitoring needs. We’ll guide you through setting up these dashboards, enabling you to visualize and analyze your metrics effectively.This document assumes you have a basic understanding of Kubernetes, EKS, and Helm. If you’re new to any of these concepts, it’s recommended to review my respective documentation before proceeding.Let’s get started with setting up Grafana on EKS with persistent storage and custom dashboards.EKS cluster IAM OIDC — EnableIAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)Enable EBS CSI Driver as AddonEnabling IAM OIDC Provider for EKS ClusterEnable IAM OIDC using Eksctl:To enable IAM OIDC provider for your EKS cluster, run the following command using CLIeksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --region <your-region> --cluster <your-cluster-name> --approveReplace <your-region> and <your-cluster-name> with your AWS region and EKS cluster name respectively.Another approach, If eksctl is difficult to use try following the below steps in UIEnable IAM OIDC using AWS UI:Under the “Configuration” tab, scroll down to the “Details” section.Click on the “Enable IAM OIDC provider” button.Enabling the EBS CSI Driver Add-on via AWS Management ConsoleChoose the EKS cluster where you want to enable the EBS CSI driver.Click on the “Add-ons” tab within your cluster’s details page.Install the EBS CSI Driver:Click “Add add-on”.Select “Amazon EBS CSI Driver” from the list of available add-ons.Choose the version you want to install and click “Install”.After installing the EBS CSI driver using the console or eksctl, verify that the driver is running correctly:kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=ebs-csi-controllerYou should see a list of running pods for the EBS CSI driver controller. With the EBS CSI driver installed and running, you’re ready to configure the StorageClass and PVC for your Grafana deployment.Creating IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)Check the Role if its already existed for the cluster, if not create one:Create IAM Role with Required Policy:Create a JSON file named trust-policy.json with the following content. This policy allows the EKS service account to assume the IAM role:{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Principal": { "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.<your-region>.amazonaws.com/id/<eks-cluster-id>" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity", "Condition": { "oidc.eks.<your-region>.amazonaws.com/id/<eks-cluster-id>:sub": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa" } ]<eks-cluster-id> with your AWS account ID, region, and EKS cluster ID respectively.Create the IAM role with the trust policy:aws iam create-role --role-name AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-policy.jsonCreate the Service Account in Kubernetes:Change the Account number where IAM role is createdeksctl create iamserviceaccount \ --name ebs-csi-controller-sa \ --namespace kube-system \ --cluster <your-cluster-name> \ --attach-role-arn arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole \ --approve \Copy the below yaml and run the following command to create the SAkubectl apply -f ebs-csi-controller-sa.yaml -n kube-system#ebs-csi-controller-sa.yaml automountServiceAccountToken: truemetadata: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/AmazonEKS_EBS_CSI_DriverRole app.kubernetes.io/component: csi-driver app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: EKS app.kubernetes.io/name: aws-ebs-csi-driver name: ebs-csi-controller-sa namespace: kube-systemClone the Code Using Feature BranchClone the Grafana Helm chart repository using the feature branch:git clone https://github.com/Ramksai07/grafana-automation.git The grafana ConfigMap is used to configure various aspects of Grafana within your Kubernetes environment. It includes settings for plugins, Grafana configuration, datasources, and dashboard providers.grafana-helm-chart-develop/templates/configmap.yamlSpecifies the Grafana plugins to be installed and used:plugins: | grafana-timestream-datasource,alexanderzobnin-zabbix-app,ClickHouse,volkovlabs-echarts-panelGrafana Configuration (grafana.ini)Configures various settings for Grafana:#make sure to use the right domain, root_url this will be configured in R53,grafana.ini: | [analytics] [grafana_net] url = https://grafana.net [log] [paths] logs = /var/log/grafana plugins = /var/lib/grafana/plugins provisioning = /etc/grafana/provisioning domain = 'grafana-test.xxx.net' root_url= 'https://grafana-test.xxx.net'Datasources Configuration (datasources.yaml)Defines data sources for Grafana: Change the sources datasources.yaml: | apiVersion: 1 - name: CloudWatch jsonData: defaultRegion: us-east-1Dashboard Providers (dashboardproviders.yaml)Specifies providers for custom dashboards:dashboardproviders.yaml: | apiVersion: 1 - name: 'custom-dashboards' folder: '' disableDeletion: false path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards foldersFromFilesStructure: true: Apply the ConfigMap to your Kubernetes cluster using kubectl apply -f grafana-configmap.yaml.Integration with Helm Charts: If using Helm, integrate this ConfigMap into your Helm deployment for Grafana to ensure consistent configuration across deployments.: Modify the respective YAML sections (plugins, grafana.ini, datasources.yaml, dashboardproviders.yaml) to reflect any changes needed in Grafana plugins, configuration settings, datasources, or dashboard providers. Apply changes using kubectl apply -f grafana-configmap.yaml.This structured approach separates the configurations for plugins, datasources, and dashboard providers, making it easier for readers to understand and update specific sections according to their requirements. Adjust paths and settings as per your environment and Grafana setup needs.Create a Namespace in KubernetesCreate a namespace for your Grafana deployment:kubectl create namespace grafana-testBuild and Apply Helm Template (Optional)If needed, build the Helm template from your cloned code and apply it to Kubernetes. Otherwise, you can directly install the Helm chart.helm template grafana-helm-chart grafana-helm-chart -n grafana-test > grafana-test.yamlVerify the generated template:kubectl apply -f grafana-test.yaml -n grafana-testTo Install Helm Chart Directly:helm install grafana-helm-chart grafana-helm-chart -n grafana-testPost Deployment VerificationAfter deploying Grafana, verify the resources and set up DNS for access.kubectl get pods -n grafana-testkubectl get pv -n grafana-testkubectl get pvc -n grafana-testkubectl get sc -n grafana-testkubectl get svc -n grafana-testGrab Load Balancer IP for DNS SetupFind the External IP or Load Balancer endpoint:kubectl get svc -n grafana-testNAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGEgrafana LoadBalancer xx.xx.xx.xx kxxxxxxxxx.elb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com 80:31139/TCP,443:32747/TCP 75mConfigure DNS in AWS Route 53Create a CNAME record pointing to the External IP or Load Balancer endpoint obtained above in AWS Route 53 for DNS resolution.Note: Remember to change the domainname in configmap.yaml in the repodomain = 'grafana-test.xxx.net'root_url= 'https://grafana-test.xxx.net'These steps outline the process from cloning your Helm chart repository, deploying Grafana using Helm, to setting up DNS for external access. Let me know if you need further customization or additional steps!

item.title: Restrict Pod Permissions To Access AWS Resources
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: K8s KUBE2IAM vs IRSA - Restrict Pod Permissions To Access AWS ResourcesWhy Use IRSA or kube2iam in Kubernetes on AWS?In Kubernetes environments hosted on AWS, securely managing IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials for pods is important. Without proper management, pods may inadvertently inherit node role permissions, potentially granting them unrestricted access across AWS resources. This scenario highlights the critical need for tools like IRSA (IAM Role for Service Accounts) and kube2iam.What Happens If We Don’t Use IRSA or Kube2iam?If IRSA or Kube2iam is not implemented:Pods inherit node role permissions:Pods running in the Kubernetes cluster will default to assuming the IAM role associated with the underlying EC2 instance (node role). This role often has broad permissions, potentially granting unrestricted access to AWS resources not required for the pod.This lack of isolation can lead to security vulnerabilities and compliance issues, as pods might gain unintended access beyond their required scope.Utilizing IRSA or kube2iam ensures that pods assume only the necessary IAM roles, following the principle of least privilege and enhancing the overall security posture of Kubernetes applications on AWSo Each pod/deployment can have its own role assigned.o Dynamic IAM role resolution based on pod annotations.o Flexibility in managing IAM roles and policies for individual pods.o No need of maintain and use SA.o Code changes on each pod/deploymento Requires comprehensive changes to Helm templates by development teams.o Introduces a performance overhead by intercepting AWS API requests.o Need to have kube2iam configured as DemonSet in the EKS cluster.o Adds complexity in managing multiple IAM roles and policies.IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)o No code change required if using the existing microservice-authentication Service Account (SA), only annotations must be updated with IAM role.o Integration with AWS IAM roles, providing secure and scalable access control.o Utilizes native AWS IAM functionalities, reducing the need for additional components.o Using a single existing SA can be less secure as all platforms will share the same permissions.o Requires careful planning to ensure that IAM roles and policies are efficiently managed.o Each pod can only be associated with a single IAM role, which may require combining multiple permissions into one role.Restrict Resources Access: IRSA or Kube2iamLimitations and Considerations of IRSA in Kubernetes:Single Service Account Association: A pod can only be associated with a single Kubernetes service account (SA) at a time. Each pod inherits IAM permissions from only one IAM role associated with its SA, limiting direct assignment of multiple SAs to a single pod. Achieve granular access control by creating separate IAM roles and associating each role with a distinct Kubernetes service account. For different sets of AWS resources, create specific IAM roles and SAs. For example, one IAM role for S3 and OpenSearch, and another for MSK, S3, and DocumentDB.IAM Role Size Limitations: Each IAM role has a maximum size limit for attached policies. Manage permissions efficiently by grouping related resources and ensuring policies do not exceed size limits. IAM roles and SAs are scoped within a Kubernetes namespace. Plan IAM role and SA configurations based on namespace requirements and pod-specific permissions within that namespace.Pod Security and Best Practices: Avoid over-assigning permissions to IAM roles associated with SAs. Follow the principle of least privilege, regularly review IAM policies, and adhere to security best practices to minimize attack surfaces.Complexity in SA Management:Consideration: Managing multiple SA and IAM Roles/policies can become complex as the number of applications and services growsIncreased complexity might lead to misconfigurations or security loopholes.aintain clear documentation and access control strategies. If your applications already use any SA,If we want to use the same SA we need to add annotations with the role. like wise if your applications doesnt have any Static SA, we can add the labelSet up an OIDC provider for your EKS cluster:export CLUSTER_NAME=your-cluster-nameeksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider --cluster $CLUSTER_NAME --approve2. Retrieve the OIDC provider URL:export OIDC_PROVIDER=$(aws eks describe-cluster --name $CLUSTER_NAME --query "cluster.identity.oidc.issuer" --output text | sed -e "s/^https:\/\///")Implementing IRSA — IAM Roles, Service Accounts, and Pods: If Pod needs only OpenSearch and S3 permissions we need to have an IAM Role with S3 and OpenSearch ServiceCreate an IAM policy for OpenSearch and S3 access:{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "es:*" "Resource": "*" ]2. Create an IAM Node Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role] and attach the policy to a IAM Role.eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-roleCreate a Trust relationship, with following code. Replace the OIDC "Statement": [ "Effect": "Allow", "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::'"$ACCOUNT_ID"':oidc-provider/'"$OIDC_PROVIDER"'" "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity", "StringEquals": { "'"$OIDC_PROVIDER":aud"'": "sts.amazonaws.com", "'"$OIDC_PROVIDER":sub"'": "system:serviceaccount:default:irsa-s3-opensearch-sa" } ]3. Create a ServiceAccount [] to use the above IAM Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role]apiVersion: v1kind: ServiceAccount name: irsa-s3-opensearch-sa annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxx:role/eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role4. Use SA in POD DeploymentapiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: pod-with-s3-opensearch-accessspec: serviceAccountName: irsa-s3-opensearch-sa containers: image: nginxIf Pod needs only MSK, S3 and DocumentDB permissions we need to have a IAM Role with S3 and OpenSearch ServiceFollow similar steps as in Scenario 1, adjusting the policy{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "s3:*", ], }}2. Create a new IAM Node Role [eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role] which are required for pod access and attach the Step 1 Policy and Trust Relation ship.eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role2. Create a ServiceAccount [] to use above IAM Role [eks-irsa-s3-opensearch-role]Create a ns as we are setting up now in Test NSapiVersion: v1kind: ServiceAccount name: irsa-msk-s3-docudb-sa annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxxx:role/eks-irsa-msk-s3-docudb-role3. Use SA in POD DeploymentapiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: pod-with-msk-s3-docudb-accessspec: serviceAccountName: irsa-msk-s3-docudb-sa containers: image: nginxLimitations and Considerations of Using kube2iamSingle IAM Role Per Pod:Limitation: A pod can only be associated with a single IAM role via annotations. You cannot directly assign multiple IAM roles to a single pod. Each pod can inherit permissions from only one IAM role specified in its annotations. Carefully design your IAM roles to combine necessary permissions or split workloads across multiple pods, each with its specific IAM role.Security Considerations:Limitation: If a pod is compromised, the attacker could potentially access all the resources that the associated IAM role permits. Ensure that the IAM roles grant the minimum necessary permissions. Follow the principle of least privilege, regularly review IAM policies, and rotate IAM credentials.IAM Role Size Limits:Consideration: Each IAM role has a maximum size limit for attached policies. Ensure your policies do not exceed these limits. Combine permissions efficiently to fit within a single role if necessary.Namespace Scope and Isolation:Consideration: kube2iam annotations are pod/deployment. Ensure roles are appropriately scoped within namespaces and that sensitive roles are not overly permissive across namespaces. Use separate namespaces to isolate environments and apply specific IAM roles to each namespace.Performance Overhead:Consideration: kube2iam introduces a performance overhead by intercepting and processing AWS API requests. This could impact the performance of applications that make frequent AWS API calls. Monitor the performance and scalability of kube2iam in your cluster and adjust resource limits as needed.Complexity in Role Management:Consideration: Managing multiple IAM roles and policies can become complex as the number of applications and services grows. Increased complexity might lead to misconfigurations or security loopholes. Maintain clear documentation and access control strategies.When a pod with a Kube2IAM annotation makes an AWS API call, the AWS SDK (or CLI) attempts to retrieve credentials from the AWS metadata service (http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/).Kube2IAM intercepts this request using iptables rules.Kube2IAM, running on the node, the IAM role specified in the pod’s annotation using the node’s IAM role (node_instance_role).Kube2IAM then returns temporary credentials for the assumed role to the pod.Example Code for implementing Kube2IAM: Prerequisites:Need to have IAM Roles created for specific services and update the trusts to have node roles to assumeNeed to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployedPurpose: Grants the pod permissions to access OpenSearch.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "es:ListDomainNames", "es:DescribeElasticsearchDomain", "es:ListElasticsearchDomainNames", "es:ESHttpPut", "es:ESHttpDelete" "Resource": "arn:aws:es:YOUR_REGION:YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:domain/YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN/*" ]Replace YOUR_REGION, YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID, and YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN with your actual values.Tighten the Action list if possible. Instead of es:ESHttpGet, etc., specify the API calls your application makes.The Resource ARN should be as specific as possible.Trust Relationship: (As previously documented, but repeating for completeness). This allows the EC2 instances (your Kubernetes nodes) to this role.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Principal": { "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" }, "Effect": "Allow", "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE" "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" ]Replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID and YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE with your actual values. YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE is the IAM role assigned to your EC2 worker nodes.Purpose: Grants the pod permissions to access OpenSearch and S3.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": [ "es:ListDomainNames", "es:DescribeElasticsearchDomain", "es:ListElasticsearchDomainNames", "es:ESHttpPut", "es:ESHttpDelete" "Resource": "arn:aws:es:YOUR_REGION:YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:domain/YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN/*" { "Action": [ "s3:GetObject", "s3:DeleteObject" "Resource": [ "arn:aws:s3:::YOUR_BUCKET", "arn:aws:s3:::YOUR_BUCKET/*" }}Replace YOUR_REGION, YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID, and YOUR_OPENSEARCH_DOMAIN with your actual values for the OpenSearch section.Replace YOUR_BUCKET with your S3 bucket name(s). If you need access to multiple buckets, add them as separate Resource entries.Again, tighten the Action list to the minimum required permissions. If the pod only from S3, remove s3:PutObject and s3:DeleteObject.Trust Relationship: Identical to k2iam-opensearch-role. It allows the EC2 instances (your Kubernetes nodes) to this role.{ "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Principal": { "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" }, "Effect": "Allow", "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/YOUR_NODE_INSTANCE_ROLE" "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" ]We can have One Default role which we can add all required services in one common IAM Role { "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-opensearch-role", "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-docudb-role", "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/k2iam-s3-role" }, "Effect": "Allow", "ec2:DescribeInstances", ], }}IAM Role’s Trust relationships:#### Trust for node roles#### Replace Node IAM Role within the EKS cluster where pod runs "Version": "2012-10-17", { "Principal": { "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" }, "Effect": "Allow", "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/NodeInstanceRole-HdShDP" "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" ]Configure KUBE2IAM :Let's configure kube2iam with example, This will create a Demonset resources which will be running on all the nodes where pods get deployed so kube2iam intercepts AWS API requests originating from pods within the Kubernetes cluster.: Let’s configure kube2iam with example, This will create a Demonset resources which will be running on all the nodes where pods get deployed So kube2iam intercepts AWS API requests originating from pods within the Kubernetes cluster.— — base-role-arn=arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/ — — default-role=k2iam-default# Source: kube2iam/templates/serviceaccount.yamlkind: ServiceAccount labels: app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam---# Source: kube2iam/templates/clusterrole.yamlapiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1metadata: app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam name: kube2iam- apiGroups: resources: - pods - list - get# Source: kube2iam/templates/clusterrolebinding.yamlapiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1kind: ClusterRoleBinding labels: app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iamroleRef: apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io kind: ClusterRolesubjects: name: kube2iam---# Source: kube2iam/templates/daemonset.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1metadata: app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam name: kube2iamspec: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam metadata: app.kubernetes.io/instance: kube2iam app.kubernetes.io/name: kube2iam spec: - args: - --node=$(NODE_NAME) - --iptables=true - --base-role-arn=arn:aws:iam::xxxxxxx:role/ - --default-role=k2iam-default - --metrics-port=8181 - --use-regional-sts-endpoint - --verbose - name: AWS_REGION - name: HOST_IP fieldRef: fieldPath: status.podIP valueFrom: apiVersion: v1 image: jtblin/kube2iam:0.11.2 imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent livenessProbe: httpGet: port: 8181 initialDelaySeconds: 30 successThreshold: 1 name: kube2iam - containerPort: 8181 protocol: TCP securityContext: terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log terminationMessagePolicy: File dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst restartPolicy: Always schedulerName: default-scheduler securityContext: {} serviceAccountName: kube2iam terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30 updateStrategy:Copy the above code to a file (kube2iam.yaml) and you can run the below commands. If you want the pods to be running on the specific node you can use the concept of taints/tolerentskubectl apply -f kube2iam.yaml -n kube-systemkubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube2iam #pods will be up and running, no of pods depending on the nodesExample Code for implementing Kube2IAM — IAM Roles, PodsScenario 1: If Pod needs only Opensearch and S3 permissions we need to have a IAM Role with OpensearchNeed to have IAM Roles created for specific services Opensearch and update the trusts to have node roles to assumeNeed to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployedAnnotate the Pod with the IAM RoleCreate a Kubernetes pod with the annotation specifying the IAM role (kube2iam-s3-role) for Opensearch access.apiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: services-pod-opensearch annotations: iam.amazonaws.com/role: k2iam-opensearch-rolespec: - name: aws-cli-test-kube2iam image: amazon/aws-cli:2.11.13 command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"] - "while true; do sleep 30; done" - name: AWS_REGION value: us-east-1 # Replace with your AWS region securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false resources: cpu: 0.5 requests: memory: 256MiOnce we exec into the pod we can check the iam role associated to the podkubectl exec -it services-test-pod-few -n test-kube2iam -- bashbash-4.2# aws sts get-caller-identity "UserId": "xxxxxx:xxxxxxx-kiam-opensearch-role", "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::xxxxxx:assumed-role/k2iam-opensearch-role/xxxxxx-kube2iam-opensearch-role"bash-4.2# curl -u xxxxx:xxxxx "https://opensearch.amazonaws.com" "name" : "xxx", "cluster_name" : "xxxxxxxxx:xxxxxxx", "cluster_uuid" : "xxxxxx", "distribution" : "opensearch", "build_type" : "tar", "build_snapshot" : false, "lucene_version" : "9.7.0" "tagline" : "The OpenSearch Project: https://opensearch.org/"An error occurred (AccessDenied) when calling the ListBuckets operation: Access DeniedIf you see when I tried to list s3 buckets, I got AccessDenied. The core issue here is that the k2iam-opensearch-role has permissions for OpenSearch. Therefore, when you tried aws s3 ls, you correctly received an AccessDenied error. This confirms that Kube2IAM is working as configured!Explanation: The pod assumed the k2iam-opensearch-role via the annotation. That role's policy didn't include S3 permissions.Scenario 2: If Pod needs only Opensearch and S3 permissionswe need to have a IAM Role with with Opensearch and s3 access binded to the roleAnnotate the Pod with the IAM RoleNeed to have IAM Roles created for specific services Opensearch and s3 and update the trusts to have node roles to assumeNeed to have Kube2IAM Demonset deployedIAM Role with multiple Servicesk2iam-opensearch-s3-role: IAM Role created, with policy having s3 and opensearch serviceAnnotate the Pod with the IAM RoleCreate a Kubernetes pod with the annotation specifying the IAM role (k2iam-opensearch-s3-role) for Opensearch access.apiVersion: v1metadata: name: services-test-pod-s3-os namespace: test iam.amazonaws.com/role: k2iam-opensearch-s3-role containers: - name: aws-cli-test-kube2iam image: amazon/aws-cli:2.11.13 command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"] args: - "while true; do sleep 30; done" env: value: us-east-1 # Replace with your AWS region allowPrivilegeEscalation: false limits: memory: 512Mi cpu: 0.25 restartPolicy: NeverOnce we exec into the pod we can check the iam role associated to the podkubectl exec -it services-test-pod-s3-os -n test-kube2iam -- bashbash-4.2# aws sts get-caller-identity{ "UserId": "xxxxxx:xxxxxx-k2iam-opensearch-s3-role", "Account": "xxxxx", "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::xxxxxx:assumed-role/k2iam-opensearch-s3-role/xxxxxx-kube2iam-opensearch-s3-role"}curl -u xxxxxx:xxxxxxx "https://xxxxxxxx.es.amazonaws.com" "name" : "xxxxx", "cluster_name" : "xxxxxxxx:xxxxxxxx", "cluster_uuid" : "xxxxxxxxxx", "distribution" : "opensearch", "build_type" : "tar", "build_snapshot" : false, "lucene_version" : "9.7.0", "minimum_wire_compatibility_version" : "7.10.0", "minimum_index_compatibility_version" : "7.0.0" "tagline" : "The OpenSearch Project: https://opensearch.org/"bash-4.2# aws s3 ls | grep test-int2021-08-26 13:28:21 test-intIn this scenario, the k2iam-opensearch-s3-role had permissions for both OpenSearch and S3. The aws s3 ls command succeeded, demonstrating that the pod could access S3.

item.title: Setting Up An Ubuntu Instance for NodeJS Apps in OVH Cloud Using Nginx, PM2 & Github Actions
item.author: Partha Roy
